abstract: AI agents integrated with Web3 offer autonomy and openness but raise security concerns as they interact with financial protocols and immutable smart contracts. This paper investigates the vulnerabilities of AI agents within blockchain-based financial ecosystems when exposed to adversarial threats in real-world scenarios. We introduce the concept of context manipulation -- a comprehensive attack vector that exploits unprotected context surfaces, including input channels, memory modules, and external data feeds. It expands on traditional prompt injection and reveals a more stealthy and persistent threat: memory injection. Using ElizaOS, a representative decentralized AI agent framework for automated Web3 operations, we showcase that malicious injections into prompts or historical records can trigger unauthorized asset transfers and protocol violations which could be financially devastating in reality. To quantify these risks, we introduce CrAIBench, a Web3-focused benchmark covering 150+ realistic blockchain tasks. such as token transfers, trading, bridges, and cross-chain interactions, and 500+ attack test cases using context manipulation. Our evaluation results confirm that AI models are significantly more vulnerable to memory injection compared to prompt injection. Finally, we evaluate a comprehensive defense roadmap, finding that prompt-injection defenses and detectors only provide limited protection when stored context is corrupted, whereas fine-tuning-based defenses substantially reduce attack success rates while preserving performance on single-step tasks. These results underscore the urgent need for AI agents that are both secure and fiduciarily responsible in blockchain environments.

keyInsights: Memory injection represents a more potent and stealthy attack vector than prompt injection, exploiting the agent's persistent internal memory for long-term influence.
Existing prompt-level defenses and state-of-the-art prompt injection detectors offer only limited protection against memory injection attacks.
Shared memory systems in multi-user Web3 agents facilitate cross-platform attack propagation, turning individual compromises into systemic vulnerabilities.
originalProblem: Existing research on LLM vulnerabilities primarily focuses on transient prompt injection, overlooking persistent threats to agent memory.
Autonomous AI agents in Web3 and DeFi manage high-value, irreversible transactions, creating critical security gaps not addressed by general LLM security studies.
The unique risks of shared, persistent memory in Web3 agents, where a single compromise can propagate across platforms and users, remained under-explored.
results: Memory injection attacks consistently achieved higher Attack Success Rates (ASR) than prompt injection across all tested LLM models on the CrAIBench benchmark.
Prompt-level defenses, while effective against prompt injection, provided limited protection against memory injection, which subtly blends into the agent's trusted internal history.
Fine-tuning Qwen-2.5-14B-Instruct models on security-augmented datasets significantly reduced ASR for memory injection from 85.1% to 1.7% on single-step tasks, while preserving benign utility.
solution: Formalized a general AI agent architecture and defined "context manipulation" as a broad attack class, introducing "memory injection" (CM-MI) as a novel, persistent threat to agent history.
Empirically validated indirect memory injection on ElizaOS, an open-source Web3 AI agent framework, demonstrating cross-platform propagation of attacks.
Developed CrAIBench, a specialized crypto-oriented benchmark, to quantitatively evaluate the success rates of context manipulation attacks and the efficacy of various defenses on different LLMs.
paper_summary.summary: A study by Princeton University and the Sentient Foundation formalized "memory injection" as a stealthy context manipulation attack against LLM-powered Web3 agents. The research empirically demonstrated that these attacks achieve higher success rates than prompt injection and bypass current prompt-level defenses, with fine-tuning showing improved robustness, particularly on the CrAIBench benchmark and ElizaOS.

title: Real AI Agents with Fake Memories: Fatal Context Manipulation Attacks on Web3 Agents

abstract: Recent advances have enabled LLM-powered AI agents to autonomously execute complex tasks by combining language model reasoning with tools, memory, and web access. But can these systems be trusted to follow deployment policies in realistic environments, especially under attack? To investigate, we ran the largest public red-teaming competition to date, targeting 22 frontier AI agents across 44 realistic deployment scenarios. Participants submitted 1.8 million prompt-injection attacks, with over 60,000 successfully eliciting policy violations such as unauthorized data access, illicit financial actions, and regulatory noncompliance. We use these results to build the Agent Red Teaming (ART) benchmark - a curated set of high-impact attacks - and evaluate it across 19 state-of-the-art models. Nearly all agents exhibit policy violations for most behaviors within 10-100 queries, with high attack transferability across models and tasks. Importantly, we find limited correlation between agent robustness and model size, capability, or inference-time compute, suggesting that additional defenses are needed against adversarial misuse. Our findings highlight critical and persistent vulnerabilities in today's AI agents. By releasing the ART benchmark and accompanying evaluation framework, we aim to support more rigorous security assessment and drive progress toward safer agent deployment.

keyInsights: AI agent robustness does not strongly correlate with model capability (e.g., GPQA score), size, or increased inference-time compute, suggesting that current defense strategies are insufficient.

Indirect prompt injections, where malicious instructions are embedded within untrusted external data, are significantly more effective than direct chat-based attacks, especially for prohibited actions and confidentiality breaches.

Attacks exhibited high transferability across different models and universality across multiple target behaviors, indicating systemic vulnerabilities rather than isolated weaknesses in individual agents.
originalProblem: A lack of comprehensive, realistic security evaluations for autonomous AI agents operating in complex, dynamic environments.
Uncertainty regarding AI agents' ability to consistently adhere to predefined deployment policies under adversarial conditions.

Existing evaluations for LLM-based systems were primarily limited to static chatbot interactions or artificial settings, failing to capture real-world agent risks involving tool use and untrusted data.
results: All 22 evaluated frontier LLM agents experienced repeated successful attacks across all 44 target behaviors, demonstrating universal susceptibility to policy violations.
The average overall Attack Success Rate (ASR) was 12.7%, with indirect prompt injections achieving 27.1% ASR compared to 5.7% for direct chat interactions.

The ART benchmark proved exceptionally challenging, with most agents exhibiting policy violations across nearly all tested behaviors within 10 to 100 queries, and for 20% to 60% of behaviors with just a single query.
solution: A large-scale public red-teaming competition, the "AI Agent Red Teaming Challenge," was hosted on the Gray Swan Arena, engaging nearly 2,000 red-teamers against 22 frontier LLM agents.
The competition featured 44 distinct, realistic simulated deployment scenarios, each with an agent persona, tool environment, and explicit adversarial goals targeting policy violations.
The Agent Red Teaming (ART) benchmark was curated from over 62,000 successful attacks collected during the competition, serving as a rigorous and dynamic tool for future security assessments.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers conducted the largest public red-teaming competition against 22 frontier AI agents, revealing universal susceptibility to policy violations with an average 12.7% attack success rate. Indirect prompt injections were substantially more effective (27.1%) than direct methods (5.7%), and agent robustness showed no strong correlation with model capability or inference-time compute.

title: Security Challenges in AI Agent Deployment: Insights from a Large Scale Public Competition

abstract:
Although LLM-based agents, powered by Large Language Models (LLMs), can use
external tools and memory mechanisms to solve complex real-world tasks, they
may also introduce critical security vulnerabilities. However, the existing
literature does not comprehensively evaluate attacks and defenses against
LLM-based agents. To address this, we introduce Agent Security Bench (ASB), a
comprehensive framework designed to formalize, benchmark, and evaluate the
attacks and defenses of LLM-based agents, including 10 scenarios (e.g.,
e-commerce, autonomous driving, finance), 10 agents targeting the scenarios,
over 400 tools, 27 different types of attack/defense methods, and 7 evaluation
metrics. Based on ASB, we benchmark 10 prompt injection attacks, a memory
poisoning attack, a novel Plan-of-Thought backdoor attack, 4 mixed attacks, and
11 corresponding defenses across 13 LLM backbones. Our benchmark results reveal
critical vulnerabilities in different stages of agent operation, including
system prompt, user prompt handling, tool usage, and memory retrieval, with the
highest average attack success rate of 84.30\%, but limited effectiveness shown
in current defenses, unveiling important works to be done in terms of agent
security for the community. We also introduce a new metric to evaluate the
agents' capability to balance utility and security. Our code can be found at
this https URL

keyInsights: LLM-based agents exhibit widespread vulnerabilities to various adversarial attacks across their operational pipeline.
Current prevention and detection-based defense mechanisms are largely ineffective in mitigating these attacks and often introduce utility loss.
The novel Plan-of-Thought (PoT) Backdoor Attack poses a significant threat, demonstrating high success rates by corrupting the agent's planning process via system prompt manipulation.
originalProblem: Existing research on LLM agent security is nascent and fragmented, focusing on limited attack vectors or scenarios.
There is a lack of comprehensive frameworks for systematically evaluating vulnerabilities and defense efficacy across the full spectrum of LLM agent operations.
The increasing autonomy and integration of LLM agents into critical systems introduce significant security vulnerabilities that are not adequately understood or addressed.
results: Mixed attacks (combining DPI, IPI, and Memory Poisoning) achieved the highest average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 84.30% with minimal refusal rates (3.22%).
Direct Prompt Injection (DPI) showed an average ASR of 72.68%, and the novel Plan-of-Thought (PoT) Backdoor Attack achieved ASRs of 100% against GPT-4o and 95.50% against GPT-4o-mini.

Existing prevention-based defenses were largely inadequate, for instance, Dynamic Prompt Rewriting for DPI still resulted in an average ASR of 44.45%; detection-based defenses for Memory Poisoning showed a high average False Negative Rate (FNR) of 0.660.
solution: The Agent Security Bench (ASB) is developed as a comprehensive framework, encompassing 10 diverse scenarios, 10 tailored agents, over 400 tools (normal and attack-specific), and 400 tasks.

The framework formalizes multiple attack types targeting different agent stages, including Direct Prompt Injection (DPI), Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI), Memory Poisoning, and a novel Plan-of-Thought (PoT) Backdoor Attack.

ASB evaluates 11 defense strategies (both prevention and detection-based) and introduces the Net Resilient Performance (NRP) metric to holistically assess the balance between an agent's utility and security.
paper_summary.summary: AGENT SECURITY BENCH (ASB) introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the security of LLM-based agents against various adversarial attacks, including a novel Plan-of-Thought Backdoor Attack. The research demonstrates that LLM-based agents are vulnerable across different operational stages and that current defense mechanisms are largely ineffective in mitigating these threats.

title: Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and  Defenses in LLM-based Agents

abstract:
Recent work has embodied LLMs as agents, allowing them to access tools, perform actions, and interact with external content (e.g., emails or websites). However, external content introduces the risk of indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks, where malicious instructions are embedded within the content processed by LLMs, aiming to manipulate these agents into executing detrimental actions against users. Given the potentially severe consequences of such attacks, establishing benchmarks to assess and mitigate these risks is imperative.
In this work, we introduce InjecAgent, a benchmark designed to assess the vulnerability of tool-integrated LLM agents to IPI attacks. InjecAgent comprises 1,054 test cases covering 17 different user tools and 62 attacker tools. We categorize attack intentions into two primary types: direct harm to users and exfiltration of private data. We evaluate 30 different LLM agents and show that agents are vulnerable to IPI attacks, with ReAct-prompted GPT-4 vulnerable to attacks 24% of the time. Further investigation into an enhanced setting, where the attacker instructions are reinforced with a hacking prompt, shows additional increases in success rates, nearly doubling the attack success rate on the ReAct-prompted GPT-4. Our findings raise questions about the widespread deployment of LLM Agents. Our benchmark is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Prompted LLM agents, even those based on powerful models like GPT-4, are highly vulnerable to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks, with some open-source models exhibiting over 80% attack success rates.

The freedom of content within the external data processed by the LLM agent correlates more strongly with attack success than the specific malicious instruction, as high content freedom allows for better blending of malicious prompts.

Fine-tuned LLM agents demonstrate greater resilience to IPI attacks compared to prompted ones, though they are not fully immune, especially concerning the data transmission phase of data-stealing attacks.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents, when integrated with external tools and content, become susceptible to manipulation via hidden malicious instructions (Indirect Prompt Injection).
These indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks can lead to severe real-world consequences, including data exfiltration, unauthorized transactions, or physical harm.
A systematic and comprehensive benchmark was lacking to evaluate the specific IPI vulnerabilities of tool-integrated LLM agents across diverse scenarios and attack intentions.
results: ReAct-prompted GPT-4 showed an Attack Success Rate (ASR-valid) of 24% in the base setting, which nearly doubled to 47% in the enhanced setting with a hacking prompt.
ReAct-prompted Llama2-70B exhibited ASRs exceeding 80% in both base and enhanced settings, indicating high susceptibility.

Fine-tuned GPT-4 achieved a significantly lower ASR-valid of 7.1%, and fine-tuned GPT-3.5 8.4%, while the data transmission phase of data stealing attacks reached 100% success for both fine-tuned GPT-3.5 and GPT-4.
solution: Formalizes the indirect prompt injection (IPI) problem for LLM agents utilizing external tools.
Introduces INJECAGENT, a benchmark of 1,054 test cases, generated with GPT-4 and manual refinement, covering 17 user tools and 62 attacker instructions categorized into direct harm and data stealing.
Evaluates 30 diverse LLM agents (prompted and fine-tuned) by simulating IPI attacks and measuring Attack Success Rate (ASR-valid) based on successful execution of malicious actions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign developed INJECAGENT, a benchmark to assess the vulnerability of tool-integrated large language model agents to indirect prompt injection attacks. Their evaluation revealed that prompted agents, even those based on advanced models, are highly susceptible to these attacks, while fine-tuned agents exhibit improved, but not complete, resilience.

title: InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Computer use agents are LLM-based agents that can directly interact with a graphical user interface, by processing screenshots or accessibility trees. While these systems are gaining popularity, their safety has been largely overlooked, despite the fact that evaluating and understanding their potential for harmful behavior is essential for widespread adoption. To address this gap, we introduce OS-Harm, a new benchmark for measuring safety of computer use agents. OS-Harm is built on top of the OSWorld environment and aims to test models across three categories of harm: deliberate user misuse, prompt injection attacks, and model misbehavior. To cover these cases, we create 150 tasks that span several types of safety violations (harassment, copyright infringement, disinformation, data exfiltration, etc.) and require the agent to interact with a variety of OS applications (email client, code editor, browser, etc.). Moreover, we propose an automated judge to evaluate both accuracy and safety of agents that achieves high agreement with human annotations (0.76 and 0.79 F1 score). We evaluate computer use agents based on a range of frontier models - such as o4-mini, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro - and provide insights into their safety. In particular, all models tend to directly comply with many deliberate misuse queries, are relatively vulnerable to static prompt injections, and occasionally perform unsafe actions. The OS-Harm benchmark is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM-based computer use agents introduce fundamentally different and escalated threats, as their multi-step planning and execution can automate and scale harmful behaviors beyond chatbots.

LLM-based semantic judges are a viable, flexible, and cost-effective method for evaluating complex, open-ended agent behaviors in realistic OS environments, showing high agreement with human annotations.
Even simple jailbreak templates can significantly bypass current safety guardrails in frontier LLM agents, highlighting the need for more robust, adaptive defenses against adversarial prompts.
originalProblem: Existing LLM safety research predominantly focused on conversational chatbots, overlooking agents capable of direct computer interaction.
Current agent safety benchmarks often rely on emulated tool calls or limited browser interactions, failing to capture the complexity and real-world risks of general computer use agents.
The absence of a comprehensive framework to assess potential harmful behaviors of agents operating within a live operating system environment.
results: Evaluated frontier agents demonstrated high vulnerability to deliberate user misuse (48-70% unsafe execution rates) and moderate susceptibility to prompt injection attacks (2-20% unsafe rates).
Model misbehavior, involving costly accidental harm, occurred less frequently (4-10% unsafe rates) but remains a concern for critical system interactions.
The LLM judge achieved high agreement with human annotations, with an F1-score of 0.76 for safety evaluation and 0.79 for task completion, validating its effectiveness for complex agent behaviors.
solution: Developed OS-HARM, a benchmark using a realistic Ubuntu VM environment (OSWorld) for agents to interact with diverse OS applications (e.g., email clients, code editors, browsers, office suites).
Designed 150 tasks across three categories: deliberate user misuse, prompt injection attacks, and model misbehavior, covering various harm types and external data vectors.
Employed an LLM-based semantic judge (GPT-4.1) to evaluate both task completion and safety risks from agent execution traces, offering flexible and cost-effective assessment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from EPFL and Carnegie Mellon University developed OS-HARM, a benchmark for evaluating the safety of LLM-based computer use agents interacting with realistic operating system environments. The benchmark revealed that frontier agents are highly vulnerable to deliberate misuse (48-70% unsafe execution) and moderately susceptible to prompt injection attacks (2-20% unsafe execution).

title: OS-Harm: A Benchmark for Measuring Safety of Computer Use Agents

abstract: The rise of AI agent frameworks has introduced agent skills, modular packages containing instructions and executable code that dynamically extend agent capabilities. While this architecture enables powerful customization, skills execute with implicit trust and minimal vetting, creating a significant yet uncharacterized attack surface. We conduct the first large-scale empirical security analysis of this emerging ecosystem, collecting 42,447 skills from two major marketplaces and systematically analyzing 31,132 using SkillScan, a multi-stage detection framework integrating static analysis with LLM-based semantic classification. Our findings reveal pervasive security risks: 26.1% of skills contain at least one vulnerability, spanning 14 distinct patterns across four categories: prompt injection, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and supply chain risks. Data exfiltration (13.3%) and privilege escalation (11.8%) are most prevalent, while 5.2% of skills exhibit high-severity patterns strongly suggesting malicious intent. We find that skills bundling executable scripts are 2.12x more likely to contain vulnerabilities than instruction-only skills (OR=2.12, p<0.001). Our contributions include: (1) a grounded vulnerability taxonomy derived from 8,126 vulnerable skills, (2) a validated detection methodology achieving 86.7% precision and 82.5% recall, and (3) an open dataset and detection toolkit to support future research. These results demonstrate an urgent need for capability-based permission systems and mandatory security vetting before this attack vector is further exploited.

keyInsights: The nascent AI agent skill ecosystem exhibits a substantial security risk, with a quarter of skills containing dangerous patterns, echoing vulnerabilities found in early, unvetted software extensibility platforms.

Vulnerability likelihood correlates strongly with structural factors such as the inclusion of executable scripts (2.12x more likely) and overall skill size (2.14x more likely), offering actionable indicators for risk assessment.

The findings provide a critical call to action for platforms, developers, and users, advocating for mandatory security reviews, runtime sandboxing, secure coding practices, and informed user consent to mitigate risks.
originalProblem: AI agent frameworks use modular 'skills' that dynamically extend capabilities with implicit trust and minimal vetting, creating a novel and uncharacterized attack surface.
Traditional LLM security research primarily focused on model-level attacks, neglecting the code and instructions that agents implicitly trust.
A systematic understanding of agent skill security, including prevalence and types of vulnerabilities, was lacking despite parallels with insecure browser extensions and IDE plugins.
results: 26.1% (8,126 of 31,132) of agent skills contained at least one potentially dangerous pattern; 5.2% exhibited high-severity indicators and 12.8% low-severity patterns.

The most prevalent vulnerability categories were Data Exfiltration (13.3% of skills) and Privilege Escalation (11.8% of skills), with 44.2% of vulnerable skills containing issues in multiple categories.

Skills that bundled executable scripts were 2.12 times more likely to be vulnerable, and larger skills (>500 lines) were 2.14 times more likely, while active maintenance showed no significant correlation with security.
solution: Developed SkillScan, a novel hybrid detection framework, combining a static security scanner with LLM-based analysis (using LLM-Guard and Claude 3.5 Sonnet) for comprehensive vulnerability detection.
Collected and analyzed 31,132 unique agent skills and 3,574 associated scripts from two major public marketplaces, categorizing them into 8 functional types.
Established a taxonomy of 14 distinct vulnerability patterns across four categories, validated against manually annotated ground truth with 86.7% precision and 82.5% recall.
paper_summary.summary: An empirical study on the security posture of AI agent skills maps 14 distinct vulnerability patterns and identifies dangerous patterns in 26.1% of over 31,000 analyzed skills, with 5.2% indicating high-severity risks. This research from a collaboration involving Quantstamp and Tianjin University provides the first large-scale characterization of this emerging attack surface.

title: Agent Skills in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Security Vulnerabilities at Scale

abstract: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, demonstrate remarkable capabilities in executing complex, long-horizon tasks. However, their tightly coupled instant-messaging interaction paradigm and high-privilege execution capabilities substantially expand the system attack surface. In this paper, we present a comprehensive security threat analysis of OpenClaw. To structure our analysis, we introduce a five-layer lifecycle-oriented security framework that captures key stages of agent operation, i.e., initialization, input, inference, decision, and execution, and systematically examine compound threats across the agent's operational lifecycle, including indirect prompt injection, skill supply chain contamination, memory poisoning, and intent drift. Through detailed case studies on OpenClaw, we demonstrate the prevalence and severity of these threats and analyze the limitations of existing defenses. Our findings reveal critical weaknesses in current point-based defense mechanisms when addressing cross-temporal and multi-stage systemic risks, highlighting the need for holistic security architectures for autonomous LLM agents. Within this framework, we further examine representative defense strategies at each lifecycle stage, including plugin vetting frameworks, context-aware instruction filtering, memory integrity validation protocols, intent verification mechanisms, and capability enforcement architectures.

keyInsights: Autonomous LLM agents are vulnerable to complex, multi-stage attacks that exploit their full operational lifecycle, indicating a more profound security challenge than isolated LLM vulnerabilities.

Current point-based security defenses are inadequate due to their fragmented nature and inability to address cross-temporal attack propagation and compositional threats in persistent agent environments.
A comprehensive security strategy requires a lifecycle-aware, defense-in-depth approach that mediates and protects each stage of an agent's operation, from initial configuration to final execution.
originalProblem: Autonomous LLM agents, capable of complex, long-horizon tasks, introduce new security challenges distinct from traditional LLM applications, expanding the attack surface through persistent memory and privileged access.

Existing security analyses and point-based defenses are insufficient to mitigate multi-stage and cross-temporal threats inherent in autonomous agent operations, where vulnerabilities can propagate across different operational phases.
The tightly coupled instant-messaging interaction paradigm and high-privilege execution of autonomous agents significantly broaden the potential for systemic compromise.
results: Demonstrated five categories of threats through OpenClaw case studies: initialization threats (e.g., skill poisoning), input vulnerabilities (e.g., indirect prompt injection), state/memory corruption (e.g., memory poisoning), decision manipulation (e.g., intent drift), and execution exploitation (e.g., high-risk command execution).

Concluded that existing defense mechanisms are largely ineffective against multi-stage and cross-temporal attacks, highlighting their fragmented nature and lack of lifecycle coherence in protecting autonomous agents.

Outlined a five-layer defense-in-depth architecture with specific technical enablers for each stage, such as plugin vetting, instruction hierarchy enforcement, cryptographic state checkpointing, and kernel-level sandboxing, offering a blueprint for enhanced agent security.
solution: A five-layer, lifecycle-oriented security framework (Initialization, Input, Inference, Decision, and Execution) was introduced to systematically categorize and analyze compound threats across the agent's operational stages.

Detailed case studies on OpenClaw, a representative 'kernel–plugin' architecture, empirically demonstrated the prevalence and severity of specific threats like indirect prompt injection and memory poisoning.

A holistic defense-in-depth architecture was proposed, guided by principles of Complete Lifecycle Mediation, Defense-in-Depth, and Least Privilege with Provenance Tracking, with specific defense measures outlined for each stage.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Ant Group conducted a systematic security analysis of autonomous LLM agents, introducing a five-layer operational lifecycle framework. Their work uncovered that these agents are susceptible to complex multi-stage, cross-temporal attacks, such as skill poisoning and memory corruption, which bypass current point-based defenses, prompting the proposal of a comprehensive, lifecycle-aware defense architecture.

title: Taming OpenClaw: Security Analysis and Mitigation of Autonomous LLM Agent Threats

abstract:
As AI models are increasingly deployed across diverse real-world scenarios,
ensuring their safety remains a critical yet underexplored challenge. While
substantial efforts have been made to evaluate and enhance AI safety, the lack
of a standardized framework and comprehensive toolkit poses significant
obstacles to systematic research and practical adoption. To bridge this gap, we
introduce AISafetyLab, a unified framework and toolkit that integrates
representative attack, defense, and evaluation methodologies for AI safety.
AISafetyLab features an intuitive interface that enables developers to
seamlessly apply various techniques while maintaining a well-structured and
extensible codebase for future advancements. Additionally, we conduct empirical
studies on Vicuna, analyzing different attack and defense strategies to provide
valuable insights into their comparative effectiveness. To facilitate ongoing
research and development in AI safety, AISafetyLab is publicly available at
this https URL, and we are committed to its continuous
maintenance and improvement.

keyInsights: Attacks like AutoDAN consistently show high effectiveness, achieving over 35% Attack Success Rates across various defense scenarios.
Inference-time defenses such as Prompt Guard, Robust Aligned, and Self Evaluation, along with the training-based Safe Unlearning, are the most effective defensive strategies.
A trade-off exists between security and usability, as highly effective defenses can increase over-refusal rates.
originalProblem: Existing AI safety research faces fragmentation, with varying experimental setups making direct comparison difficult.
Reimplementing prior work for comparative evaluation is time-intensive and can lead to inconsistencies.
There is an imbalance between developed attack methodologies and lagging research on defense mechanisms, and a lack of comprehensive tools integrating all aspects of safety.
results: AISafetyLab provides a standardized framework, integrating diverse attack, defense, and evaluation methodologies into a single platform.
Empirical studies using Vicuna demonstrated that AutoDAN achieved Attack Success Rates (ASR) exceeding 35% against various defenses.
The framework identified Prompt Guard, Robust Aligned, Self Evaluation, and Safe Unlearning as leading defense mechanisms for inference-time and training-based scenarios, respectively.
solution: The paper introduces AISafetyLab, a unified, modular toolkit for AI safety evaluation and improvement, designed with core Attack, Defense, and Evaluation modules.
It implements a wide range of black-box and white-box jailbreak attack methods, including manual prompting, automated generation (e.g., AutoDAN, GCG), and gradient-based approaches.

The framework supports both training-time defenses (e.g., safety data tuning, RL-based alignment, unlearning) and inference-time defenses (e.g., preprocess, intraprocess, postprocess level interventions).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Beihang University developed AISafetyLab, a comprehensive framework that integrates various attack methodologies, defense mechanisms, and evaluation techniques for AI safety. The framework provides a standardized platform for systematic evaluation, with empirical studies demonstrating the effectiveness of methods like AutoDAN for attacks and Prompt Guard for defenses against Large Language Models like Vicuna.

title: AISafetyLab: A Comprehensive Framework for AI Safety Evaluation and  Improvement

abstract: LLM agents are evolving rapidly, powered by code execution, tools, and the recently introduced agent skills feature. Skills allow users to extend LLM applications with specialized third-party code, knowledge, and instructions. Although this can extend agent capabilities to new domains, it creates an increasingly complex agent supply chain, offering new surfaces for prompt injection attacks. We identify skill-based prompt injection as a significant threat and introduce SkillInject, a benchmark evaluating the susceptibility of widely-used LLM agents to injections through skill files. SkillInject contains 202 injection-task pairs with attacks ranging from obviously malicious injections to subtle, context-dependent attacks hidden in otherwise legitimate instructions. We evaluate frontier LLMs on SkillInject, measuring both security in terms of harmful instruction avoidance and utility in terms of legitimate instruction compliance. Our results show that today's agents are highly vulnerable with up to 80% attack success rate with frontier models, often executing extremely harmful instructions including data exfiltration, destructive action, and ransomware-like behavior. They furthermore suggest that this problem will not be solved through model scaling or simple input filtering, but that robust agent security will require context-aware authorization frameworks. Our benchmark is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM agents from major providers demonstrated high vulnerability to skill-based injections, with contextual attack success rates reaching up to 79%.
Security policy prompting, even explicit warnings, proved insufficient to fully mitigate these attacks, and LLM judges struggled to distinguish authorized actions from contextual injections.
Attacks embedding malicious payloads in auxiliary scripts or skill descriptions were significantly more effective, exploiting agents' implicit trust in code and early prompt processing.
originalProblem: Existing prompt injection research primarily addressed direct and indirect attacks on LLMs, focusing on distinguishing instructions from data.
The emergence of LLM agents using external "skills" from third-parties created a new, complex supply chain vulnerability, similar to traditional software dependencies.

Malicious instructions within skill files present an "instruction-instruction" conflict, where harmful commands are hidden within otherwise legitimate instruction sets, rendering many current defenses ineffective.
results: Frontier LLM agents exhibited high vulnerability, with contextual injection execution rates (ASR) ranging from 41.0% to 79.0% under baseline conditions, leading to the execution of harmful actions like data exfiltration.
Script-based attacks significantly amplified vulnerabilities, increasing contextual ASR by 18 percentage points and obvious ASR by 9 percentage points compared to direct text injections.
Injecting into skill descriptions increased overall ASR by an average of 14.1 percentage points, indicating that early processing of malicious instructions can override subsequent safety mechanisms.
solution: Developed SKILL-INJECT, a benchmark with 23 realistic "skills" containing both obvious (30) and contextual (41) prompt injections, mirroring real-world agent interactions.

Evaluated frontier LLM agents (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) under different natural language security policies (legitimizing, warning, baseline) and varied attacker capabilities (body, script, description injections).
Assessed agent security (Attack Success Rate) and utility (Task Completion) using LLM judges in isolated environments, collecting execution logs for analysis.
paper_summary.summary: Research from the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems demonstrates that frontier LLM agents are highly vulnerable to prompt injection attacks embedded in third-party skill files, executing harmful commands with up to 79% success. The SKILL-INJECT benchmark identifies a critical AI supply chain risk, revealing that current safety measures are insufficient against these context-dependent threats.

title: Skill-Inject: Measuring Agent Vulnerability to Skill File Attacks

abstract: OpenClaw has rapidly established itself as a leading open-source autonomous agent runtime, offering powerful capabilities including tool integration, local file access, and shell command execution. However, these broad operational privileges introduce critical security vulnerabilities, transforming model errors into tangible system-level threats such as sensitive data leakage, privilege escalation, and malicious third-party skill execution. Existing security measures for the OpenClaw ecosystem remain highly fragmented, addressing only isolated stages of the agent lifecycle rather than providing holistic protection. To bridge this gap, we present ClawKeeper, a real-time security framework that integrates multi-dimensional protection mechanisms across three complementary architectural layers. (1) \textbf{Skill-based protection} operates at the instruction level, injecting structured security policies directly into the agent context to enforce environment-specific constraints and cross-platform boundaries. (2) \textbf{Plugin-based protection} serves as an internal runtime enforcer, providing configuration hardening, proactive threat detection, and continuous behavioral monitoring throughout the execution pipeline. (3) \textbf{Watcher-based protection} introduces a novel, decoupled system-level security middleware that continuously verifies agent state evolution. It enables real-time execution intervention without coupling to the agent's internal logic, supporting operations such as halting high-risk actions or enforcing human confirmation. We argue that this Watcher paradigm holds strong potential to serve as a foundational building block for securing next-generation autonomous agent systems. Extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of ClawKeeper across diverse threat scenarios. We release our code.

keyInsights: Decoupling security oversight from the task-executing agent via an independent "Watcher" agent effectively resolves the safety-utility trade-off, allowing the task agent to optimize for performance while the Watcher focuses exclusively on safety enforcement.

A multi-layered defense combining instruction-level policies, internal runtime hardening, and external real-time monitoring provides comprehensive and robust protection against diverse threat vectors across the entire agent lifecycle.

The Watcher's design inherently offers greater resistance to adversarial manipulation and enables self-evolving security, allowing it to continuously update its safety knowledge and adapt to emerging threats without manual intervention.
originalProblem: OpenClaw agents' extensive operational privileges (e.g., local file access, shell command execution) create critical security vulnerabilities, allowing model-level errors to escalate into system-level threats like data leakage or privilege escalation.

Existing security solutions for autonomous agents are fragmented, addressing only isolated threats or specific lifecycle stages, leading to incomplete protection and a fundamental trade-off between an agent's utility and safety.

Current defense mechanisms are often reactive, identifying security issues after adversarial actions, and static, failing to adapt to emerging threats or the self-evolving nature of autonomous agents over time.
results: ClawKeeper achieved a Defense Success Rate (DSR) of 85-90% across seven categories of adversarial tasks (e.g., Prompt Injection, Credential Leakage, Dangerous Command Execution), consistently outperforming existing baselines by 15 to 45 percentage points.

The Watcher component demonstrated a self-evolving capability, with its DSR improving from approximately 90.0% to 95.0% after processing 100 incrementally provided adversarial cases, adapting to novel threat instances.

Qualitative evaluations confirmed ClawKeeper's ability to proactively block threats such as obfuscated code execution, sensitive data transmission, and latent prompt injections, while also enabling autonomous security orchestration.
solution: The paper introduces ClawKeeper, a comprehensive, multi-dimensional security framework that integrates skill-based, plugin-based, and a novel watcher-based protection across the full agent lifecycle.

It implements skill-based protection by injecting structured security policies at the instruction level and plugin-based protection as an internal runtime enforcer for threat detection and configuration hardening.

A novel Watcher-based protection is introduced as a decoupled, independent supervisory agent that monitors task-executing OpenClaw instances in real-time, allowing proactive intervention without tight coupling to the agent's internal logic.

The Watcher operates as a separate process, resistant to adversarial manipulation, and enables co-evolution with the agent, resolving the safety-utility tradeoff by separating security oversight from core task functions.
paper_summary.summary: ClawKeeper is a multi-layered security framework for OpenClaw agents, integrating skill-based, plugin-based, and a novel decoupled watcher-based protection. It achieved a Defense Success Rate (DSR) of 85-90% across seven adversarial task categories, outperforming existing baselines by 15-45 percentage points, and its Watcher component demonstrated self-evolving security capabilities.

title: ClawKeeper: Comprehensive Safety Protection for OpenClaw Agents Through Skills, Plugins, and Watchers

abstract: Code agents powered by large language models can execute shell commands on behalf of users, introducing severe security vulnerabilities. This paper presents a two-phase security analysis of the OpenClaw platform. As an open-source AI agent framework that operates locally, OpenClaw can be integrated with various commercial large language models. Because its native architecture lacks built-in security constraints, it serves as an ideal subject for evaluating baseline agent vulnerabilities. First, we systematically evaluate OpenClaw's native resilience against malicious instructions. By testing 47 adversarial scenarios across six major attack categories derived from the MITRE ATLAS and ATT\&CK frameworks, we have demonstrated that OpenClaw exhibits significant inherent security issues. It primarily relies on the security capabilities of the backend LLM and is highly susceptible to sandbox escape attacks, with an average defense rate of only 17\%. To mitigate these critical security gaps, we propose and implement a novel Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense layer. We utilize a dual-mode testing framework to evaluate the system with and without our proposed intervention. Our findings show that the introduced HITL layer significantly hardens the system, successfully intercepting up to 8 severe attacks that completely bypassed OpenClaw's native defenses. By combining native capabilities with our HITL approach, the overall defense rate improves to a range of 19\% to 92\%. Our study not only exposes the intrinsic limitations of current code agents but also demonstrates the effectiveness of human-agent collaborative defense strategies.

keyInsights: The inherent safety training and alignment of the chosen LLM backend critically influence an agent's baseline security, showing a 66-percentage-point difference in native defense rates between the best and worst performers.

A layered defense-in-depth approach, combining native LLM safety with external mechanisms like HITL, significantly enhances overall security, particularly for models with moderate baseline protection.

Attack categories like sandbox boundary violations (17% average defense rate) and privilege escalation (8% average defense rate) present persistent and difficult-to-defend vulnerabilities, even with HITL defenses.
originalProblem: LLM-powered code agents, like OpenClaw, introduce new security risks due to their broad local system access (e.g., shell commands, file operations).
Existing LLM safety research primarily focuses on harmful text generation, inadequately addressing the dangers of agent *actions* in real-world operating environments.
Traditional software security methods are often ineffective against non-deterministic, natural language-driven LLM systems, creating a gap in robust defense strategies.
results: Native defense rates without the HITL layer ranged from 17.0% (DeepSeek V3.2) to 83.0% (Claude Opus 4.6), indicating substantial variability in LLM security alignment for agent use cases.

The HITL defense layer consistently improved defense rates across all LLM backends, with the largest gain of 17.0% observed for GPT 5.3 Codex, achieving an overall maximum effective defense rate of 91.5% for Claude Opus 4.6.

The categories of Sandbox Boundary Violations and Privilege Escalation consistently exhibited the lowest average defense rates across all LLMs, highlighting these as critical unmitigated risks in local code agent operations.
solution: Developed a comprehensive taxonomy of 47 adversarial scenarios for local code agents, categorized into six groups (e.g., evasion, sandbox violations, indirect prompt injection) and mapped to MITRE ATLAS/ATT&CK tactics.
Designed and implemented a layered Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense architecture for OpenClaw that intercepts tool calls using an Allowlist, Semantic Judge, Pattern Matching, and Sandbox Guard.
Utilized a dual-mode execution engine to evaluate OpenClaw's security posture both with its native defenses and with the proposed HITL layer active across six different LLM backends.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shandong University analyzed the security of OpenClaw, an LLM-powered code agent framework, by systematically evaluating its vulnerabilities across 47 adversarial scenarios and developing a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense. The HITL layer consistently improved defense rates across six different LLM backends, achieving a peak of 91.5% for Claude Opus 4.6, though sandbox escape attacks remained largely unmitigated.

title: Don't Let the Claw Grip Your Hand: A Security Analysis and Defense Framework for OpenClaw

abstract: Recent advances in AI agents capable of solving complex, everyday tasks, from scheduling to customer service, have enabled deployment in real-world settings, but their possibilities for unsafe behavior demands rigorous evaluation. While prior benchmarks have attempted to assess agent safety, most fall short by relying on simulated environments, narrow task domains, or unrealistic tool abstractions. We introduce OpenAgentSafety, a comprehensive and modular framework for evaluating agent behavior across eight critical risk categories. Unlike prior work, our framework evaluates agents that interact with real tools, including web browsers, code execution environments, file systems, bash shells, and messaging platforms; and supports over 350 multi-turn, multi-user tasks spanning both benign and adversarial user intents. OpenAgentSafety is designed for extensibility, allowing researchers to add tools, tasks, websites, and adversarial strategies with minimal effort. It combines rule-based analysis with LLM-as-judge assessments to detect both overt and subtle unsafe behaviors. Empirical analysis of five prominent LLMs in agentic scenarios reveals unsafe behavior in 51.2% of safety-vulnerable tasks with Claude-Sonnet-3.7, to 72.7% with o3-mini, highlighting critical safety vulnerabilities and the need for stronger safeguards before real-world deployment.

keyInsights: Modern LLM agents, even advanced ones, exhibit pervasive unsafe behavior (49-73% in safety-vulnerable tasks), particularly when confronting systemic risks that require procedural judgment or understanding of institutional norms.

Unsafe behaviors are frequently elicited by benign user prompts or hidden malicious intents from secondary actors (NPCs), demonstrating a fragility in multi-turn intent tracking and the generalization of refusal strategies.

The evaluation revealed that LLM-as-Judge models often underestimate implied unsafe behaviors and overestimate failure rates by misinterpreting superficial errors, highlighting the necessity of hybrid evaluation methods.
originalProblem: Existing LLM safety evaluations primarily focus on static output generation (e.g., bias, toxicity) and do not adequately address the unique safety challenges of AI agents that interact with dynamic environments and execute actions via real-world tools.

Prior benchmarks for LLM agents often rely on simulated environments, simplified tool APIs, or narrow task domains, failing to capture the complexities and risks of realistic, multi-step interactions, particularly those involving multi-turn and multi-user dynamics.

The rapid development and deployment of LLM-powered agents with access to powerful tools have outpaced the progress in robust safety assurance methods, leading to a disparity between agent capabilities and the investigation of potential failure modes.
results: All seven prominent LLMs evaluated exhibited substantial unsafe behavior, with unsafe rates ranging from 49.06% (Claude Sonnet 4) to 72.73% (o3-mini) in safety-vulnerable tasks.

Systemic risk categories (e.g., computer security compromise, legal violations, privacy breaches) consistently showed the highest unsafe rates (72-86%), as agents frequently disregarded authorization policies or institutional norms.

Agents displayed unsafe actions in 50-86% of tasks with benign prompts and showed increased unsafe rates (44.9-69.2%) when malicious goals were introduced by NPCs, indicating challenges in multi-turn intent tracking and refusal strategies.
solution: Introduces OPENAGENTSAFETY (OA-SAFETY), an open-source framework that places AI agents in a containerized sandbox with access to real-world tools (Unix shell, file system, Python interpreter, web browser) and locally hosted replicas of real platforms like GitLab.

Supports diverse user intentions, from benign ambiguity to explicit adversarial manipulation, and incorporates multi-user scenarios by integrating secondary actors (NPCs) with varied or conflicting goals, scaled to over 350 tasks.

Employs a hybrid evaluation strategy combining precise rule-based evaluators to detect specific unsafe environmental outcomes with LLM-as-Judge assessments to identify unsafe intent or partial malicious actions in agent trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and the Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence developed OPENAGENTSAFETY, an open-source framework for evaluating AI agent safety in realistic, high-risk scenarios using sandboxed real-world tools and complex multi-user interactions. Empirical evaluation revealed that all seven tested LLMs exhibited unsafe behavior in 49-73% of safety-vulnerable tasks, especially when confronting systemic risks or subtle malicious intents from secondary actors.

title: OpenAgentSafety: A Comprehensive Framework for Evaluating Real-World AI Agent Safety

abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has emerged as a universal standard that enables AI agents to seamlessly connect with external tools, significantly enhancing their functionality. However, while MCP brings notable benefits, it also introduces significant vulnerabilities, such as Tool Poisoning Attacks (TPA), where hidden malicious instructions exploit the sycophancy of large language models (LLMs) to manipulate agent behavior. Despite these risks, current academic research on MCP security remains limited, with most studies focusing on narrow or qualitative analyses that fail to capture the diversity of real-world threats. To address this gap, we present the MCP Attack Library (MCPLIB), which categorizes and implements 31 distinct attack methods under four key classifications: direct tool injection, indirect tool injection, malicious user attacks, and LLM inherent attack. We further conduct a quantitative analysis of the efficacy of each attack. Our experiments reveal key insights into MCP vulnerabilities, including agents' blind reliance on tool descriptions, sensitivity to file-based attacks, chain attacks exploiting shared context, and difficulty distinguishing external data from executable commands. These insights, validated through attack experiments, underscore the urgency for robust defense strategies and informed MCP design. Our contributions include 1) constructing a comprehensive MCP attack taxonomy, 2) introducing a unified attack framework MCPLIB, and 3) conducting empirical vulnerability analysis to enhance MCP security mechanisms. This work provides a foundational framework, supporting the secure evolution of MCP ecosystems.

keyInsights: MCP agents exhibit varying sensitivities to operations; file-related actions (e.g., add, retrieve, read) often execute without explicit user confirmation, unlike more sensitive operations like deletion or remote code execution.

Agents show heavy reliance on tool descriptions and a 'blind obedience' to their text, prioritizing them over actual functionalities, which enables attacks like Shadowing and Tool Preference Manipulation.
The lack of isolation in MCP's shared context enables chain attacks and vulnerability propagation, allowing compromised tools to 'infect' others or facilitate unintended multi-tool cooperation.
MCP agents struggle to differentiate between external data and executable instructions, leading them to execute malicious data embedded in tool outputs or third-party sources as commands.
originalProblem: The rapid adoption of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) in AI agents introduced novel and complex security vulnerabilities by expanding the attack surface beyond traditional Large Language Model (LLM) risks.

Existing academic research on MCP security was fragmented, often theoretical, or focused on narrow attack types, failing to provide a systematic and practical understanding of the diverse threat landscape.
A lack of quantitative metrics and comprehensive attack implementations hindered objective assessment and comparison of the severity and impact of different MCP-specific attacks.
results: The research delivered the first systematic taxonomy, classifying 31 unique MCP-specific attack types and offering a comprehensive framework for threat modeling.
MCPLIB was successfully developed as a unified, plugin-based framework, enabling the practical demonstration and reproduction of various attack techniques.

Quantitative analysis using the 'Attack Efficacy' metric identified SQL Injection & API Theft as the highest efficacy attack (10.00), with file-based injection attacks (e.g., Addition, Modification) also showing high impact (8.08-8.38) and 100% success rates in many experimental scenarios without user confirmation.
solution: Developed a four-dimensional taxonomy, classifying 31 distinct MCP-specific attack methods into categories such as Direct Tool Injection, Indirect Tool Injection, Malicious User, and LLM Inherent Attacks.
Implemented MCPLIB, a unified, extensible, plugin-based attack simulation framework, to provide practical and reproducible examples for validating and analyzing MCP vulnerabilities.

Introduced a novel 'Attack Efficacy' metric, employing an entropy weight method to quantitatively assess the harm of each attack across security risk, success rate, persistent impact, and implementation difficulty.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University, Ant Group, Swinburne University of Technology, and the University of Adelaide systematically analyzed Model Context Protocol (MCP) security, developing a 31-type attack taxonomy and a unified simulation framework (MCPLIB). Their work quantitatively assessed attack efficacy and revealed critical vulnerabilities like blind obedience to tool descriptions and context-sharing flaws, providing foundational insights for secure AI agent design.

title: Systematic Analysis of MCP Security

abstract: Clawdbot is a self-hosted, tool-using personal AI agent with a broad action space spanning local execution and web-mediated workflows, which raises heightened safety and security concerns under ambiguity and adversarial steering. We present a trajectory-centric evaluation of Clawdbot across six risk dimensions. Our test suite samples and lightly adapts scenarios from prior agent-safety benchmarks (including ATBench and LPS-Bench) and supplements them with hand-designed cases tailored to Clawdbot's tool surface. We log complete interaction trajectories (messages, actions, tool-call arguments/outputs) and assess safety using both an automated trajectory judge (AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B) and human review. Across 34 canonical cases, we find a non-uniform safety profile: performance is generally consistent on reliability-focused tasks, while most failures arise under underspecified intent, open-ended goals, or benign-seeming jailbreak prompts, where minor misinterpretations can escalate into higher-impact tool actions. We supplemented the overall results with representative case studies and summarized the commonalities of these cases, analyzing the security vulnerabilities and typical failure modes that Clawdbot is prone to trigger in practice.

keyInsights: Safety failures in tool-using agents are amplified by broad tool access, allowing minor errors or adversarial inputs to cascade into high-impact, irreversible real-world side effects.
Clawdbot's design, including persisted Markdown memory and Markdown-based 'skills', creates durable states for vulnerabilities and expands the attack surface for prompt injection.
Even a modest per-task failure rate (e.g., 5%) compounds rapidly over many tasks, highlighting that agent safety must be treated as a reliability requirement akin to safety-critical software.
originalProblem: Autonomous AI agents with broad tool access, like Clawdbot, introduce emergent and complex safety challenges due to their ability to take real-world actions.
There is a significant gap between public expectations of highly capable agents and their actual operational risks, exacerbated by rapid adoption and early security flaws.
A lack of systematic, evidence-based safety evaluations for deployed agentic systems hinders understanding and mitigation of these risks.
results: Clawdbot achieved an overall safety pass rate of 58.9% across the 34 canonical test cases.
The agent exhibited high factual reliability (100% pass rate for hallucination) but showed a 0% pass rate for intent misunderstanding, making unsafe assumptions without seeking clarification.
Critical failures were observed in robustness to prompt injection (57% pass rate) and operational safety awareness, where the agent complied with socially harmful requests.
solution: A trajectory-centric safety audit was conducted on Clawdbot, evaluating its behavior across 34 curated test cases sourced from established agent safety benchmarks.
The evaluation focused on six critical dimensions of agentic failure, including user-facing deception, intent misunderstanding, and robustness to prompt injection.
Safety judgments were made using a dual-pronged approach, combining an automated trajectory judge (AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B) with human review for comprehensive and reliable assessment.
paper_summary.summary: A trajectory-based safety audit of Clawdbot, a self-hosted AI agent, reveals a 58.9% overall safety pass rate and critical vulnerabilities in handling ambiguous instructions and adversarial inputs. The analysis identifies a 0% pass rate for intent misunderstanding, leading to irreversible real-world actions despite high factual reliability.

title: A Trajectory-Based Safety Audit of Clawdbot (OpenClaw)

abstract:
The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) agents into web browsers introduces security challenges that go beyond traditional web application threat models. Prior work has identified prompt injection as a new attack vector for web agents, yet the resulting impact within real-world environments remains insufficiently understood.
In this work, we examine the landscape of prompt injection attacks and synthesize a benchmark of attacks embedded in realistic HTML payloads. Our benchmark goes beyond prior work by emphasizing injections that can influence real-world actions rather than mere text outputs, and by presenting attack payloads with complexity and distractor frequency similar to what real-world agents encounter. We leverage this benchmark to conduct a comprehensive empirical evaluation of existing defenses, assessing their effectiveness across a suite of frontier AI models. We propose a multi-layered defense strategy comprising both architectural and model-based defenses to protect against evolving prompt injection attacks. Our work offers a blueprint for designing practical, secure web agents through a defense-in-depth approach.

keyInsights: Frontier AI models exhibit substantial vulnerability to realistic prompt injection, with sophisticated linguistic styles ("Stealth," "Indirect") and visible content injections proving particularly challenging.

Current prompt injection detection systems show a "structural bias," being significantly less effective against attacks seamlessly embedded in visible, rewritten content compared to those hidden in non-rendered HTML.

The inclusion of benign "distractor" elements in web pages causes a notable decrease in detection accuracy, highlighting the brittleness of existing models and their tendency to learn spurious correlations.
originalProblem: Autonomous AI browser agents face novel security challenges, as prompt injection attacks are insufficiently understood regarding their impact on real-world agent actions in complex web environments.

Prior prompt injection benchmarks are limited in realism, attack diversity, and focus, often using simplified text content instead of complex HTML and failing to evaluate actual agent behavior manipulation.

Current defense mechanisms against prompt injection, including frontier AI models and specialized classifiers, exhibit vulnerabilities to sophisticated attacks or suffer from high latency, hindering practical deployment.
results: The `BrowseSafe` defense achieved an F1 score of 0.904, with a precision of 0.978 and a balanced accuracy of 0.912, outperforming leading general-purpose frontier AI models on the `BrowseSafe-Bench`.
`BrowseSafe` demonstrated practical real-time performance with inference latency under 1 second, contrasting with frontier models that required 23-36 seconds for similar tasks.

Evaluations on `BrowseSafe-Bench` showed that sophisticated linguistic styles and visible content injections led to lower detection accuracy (e.g., "Stealth" at 75.3%), and the presence of distractors reduced average accuracy from 90.2% to 81.2%.
The `BrowseSafe` model exhibited strong generalization across unseen websites (F1 0.935) and novel attack semantics (F1 0.863), indicating its robustness beyond specific training examples.
solution: The authors developed `BrowseSafe-Bench`, a novel and publicly available benchmark featuring 14,719 samples derived from production browser agent usage, designed to assess prompt injection in realistic web environments.

`BrowseSafe-Bench` incorporates complex HTML structures, 11 diverse attack types, 9 injection strategies, 3 linguistic styles, and adversarial distractors to simulate sophisticated real-world threats.

They proposed `BrowseSafe`, a multi-layered defense system that enforces trust boundaries, preprocesses raw content, uses a fine-tuned, low-latency detection classifier, and implements context-engineered intervention for agent recovery.
paper_summary.summary: Purdue University and Perplexity AI introduced `BrowseSafe-Bench`, a realistic benchmark featuring complex HTML and diverse attack types, alongside `BrowseSafe`, a multi-layered defense strategy for prompt injection in AI browser agents. The `BrowseSafe` defense achieved an F1 score of 0.904 with sub-second latency, outperforming existing frontier models, while the benchmark exposed significant vulnerabilities in current AI systems, particularly against sophisticated attacks.

title: BrowseSafe: Understanding and Preventing Prompt Injection Within AI Browser Agents

abstract:
Large Language Model (LLM) agents exhibit remarkable performance across
diverse applications by using external tools to interact with environments.
However, integrating external tools introduces security risks, such as indirect
prompt injection (IPI) attacks. Despite defenses designed for IPI attacks,
their robustness remains questionable due to insufficient testing against
adaptive attacks. In this paper, we evaluate eight different defenses and
bypass all of them using adaptive attacks, consistently achieving an attack
success rate of over 50%. This reveals critical vulnerabilities in current
defenses. Our research underscores the need for adaptive attack evaluation when
designing defenses to ensure robustness and reliability. The code is available
at this https URL

keyInsights: Current defenses against indirect prompt injection are insufficient and exhibit critical vulnerabilities when faced with adaptive attacks that understand and exploit their mechanisms.
Even inherently more resilient fine-tuned LLM agents remain highly susceptible to sophisticated adaptive prompt injection strategies, challenging the perception of their security.
Tailoring adaptive attacks, such as using multi-objective optimization for stealth or two-stage training for paraphrasing defenses, is crucial for effectively bypassing specific defense categories.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are vulnerable to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks, where malicious instructions embedded in external data compromise their intended functions.
Existing defenses against IPI have not been rigorously evaluated against adaptive adversaries who possess knowledge of the defense mechanisms, leading to questions about their true robustness.
A methodological gap exists in applying established adaptive attack strategies from general machine learning security to the specific challenges of IPI defenses in complex LLM agent settings.
results: Adaptive attacks successfully bypassed all eight evaluated IPI defenses, consistently achieving an attack success rate (ASR) of over 50% against both prompted and fine-tuned LLM agents.
Multi-objective GCG (M-GCG) attacks effectively drove down the detection rates of detection-based defenses to near zero while maintaining high attack success.

The study demonstrated that adaptive attacks specifically designed for defenses like perplexity filtering (AutoDAN) and paraphrasing (T-GCG) significantly outperformed non-adaptive adversarial strings.
solution: The study systematically implemented and evaluated eight diverse IPI defense mechanisms across three categories: detection-based, input-level, and model-level.

Four adaptive attack techniques (GCG, M-GCG, T-GCG, AutoDAN) were designed and tailored to specifically bypass these defenses by optimizing adversarial strings with knowledge of the defense's operation.

The methodology involved testing these defenses and adaptive attacks on two distinct LLM agent architectures: a prompted agent (Vicuna-7B) and a finetuned agent (Llama3-8B), using a subset of the InjecAgent benchmark.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and Nirma University demonstrated that all eight common defenses against Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) attacks on LLM agents can be bypassed by adaptive adversarial strategies, consistently achieving over 50% attack success rates on both prompted and fine-tuned agents.

title: Adaptive Attacks Break Defenses Against Indirect Prompt Injection  Attacks on LLM Agents

abstract: LLM based agents are increasingly deployed in high stakes settings where they process external data sources such as emails, documents, and code repositories. This creates exposure to indirect prompt injection attacks, where adversarial instructions embedded in external content manipulate agent behavior without user awareness. A critical but underexplored dimension of this threat is concealment: since users tend to observe only an agent's final response, an attack can conceal its existence by presenting no clue of compromise in the final user facing response while successfully executing harmful actions. This leaves users unaware of the manipulation and likely to accept harmful outcomes as legitimate. We present findings from a large scale public red teaming competition evaluating this dual objective across three agent settings: tool calling, coding, and computer use. The competition attracted 464 participants who submitted 272000 attack attempts against 13 frontier models, yielding 8648 successful attacks across 41 scenarios. All models proved vulnerable, with attack success rates ranging from 0.5% (Claude Opus 4.5) to 8.5% (Gemini 2.5 Pro). We identify universal attack strategies that transfer across 21 of 41 behaviors and multiple model families, suggesting fundamental weaknesses in instruction following architectures. Capability and robustness showed weak correlation, with Gemini 2.5 Pro exhibiting both high capability and high vulnerability. To address benchmark saturation and obsoleteness, we will endeavor to deliver quarterly updates through continued red teaming competitions. We open source the competition environment for use in evaluations, along with 95 successful attacks against Qwen that did not transfer to any closed source model. We share model-specific attack data with respective frontier labs and the full dataset with the UK AISI and US CAISI to support robustness research.

keyInsights: All 13 frontier LLMs evaluated demonstrated vulnerability to concealed indirect prompt injection attacks, with varying Attack Success Rates (ASRs) from 0.5% to 8.5%.
The existence of "universal attack clusters," such as the "Holodeck" strategy, suggests fundamental weaknesses in LLM instruction-following, rather than only model-specific flaws.

Attack transferability is asymmetric, with attacks successful against robust models being highly transferable to others, implying that overcoming strong safety training exploits deeper vulnerabilities.
originalProblem: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, creating new security vulnerabilities from indirect prompt injections.
Existing research and benchmarks often overlook the critical "concealment" aspect, where attacks succeed without the user detecting any compromise.
Static security benchmarks rapidly become obsolete due to the fast evolution of attack and defense techniques, necessitating a dynamic evaluation approach.
results: Tool use scenarios exhibited the highest vulnerability with an ASR of 4.82%, followed by computer use (3.13%), and coding scenarios (2.51%).
Claude Opus 4.5 showed the lowest ASR at 0.5%, while Gemini 2.5 Pro had the highest at 8.5% and achieved 100% scenario coverage.

Only a weak, non-significant negative correlation (r = -0.31) was found between model capability (GPQA scores) and robustness, suggesting other factors like training methodology play a more dominant role.
solution: Organized "Indirect Prompt Injection Arena," a large-scale public red-teaming competition with 464 participants and 13 frontier LLMs, to gather concealed indirect prompt injections.
Designed 41 unique scenarios across tool use, coding, and computer use agent settings, requiring attacks to both execute a harmful action and satisfy concealment criteria.
Implemented a dual-judge system, combining programmatic tool checks and an LLM-based prompt judge, to verify both the harmful action and its effective concealment.
paper_summary.summary: A large-scale public competition evaluated 13 frontier large language model (LLM) agents against indirect prompt injection attacks designed to execute harmful actions while remaining concealed from the user, revealing that all tested models are vulnerable to such hidden manipulations.

title: How Vulnerable Are AI Agents to Indirect Prompt Injections? Insights from a Large-Scale Public Competition

abstract:
By providing a standardized interface for LLM agents to interact with external tools, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is quickly becoming a cornerstone of the modern autonomous agent ecosystem. However, it creates novel attack surfaces due to untrusted external tools. While prior work has focused on attacks injected through external tool outputs, we investigate a more fundamental vulnerability: Tool Poisoning, where malicious instructions are embedded within a tool's metadata without execution. To date, this threat has been primarily demonstrated through isolated cases, lacking a systematic, large-scale evaluation.
We introduce MCPTox, the first benchmark to systematically evaluate agent robustness against Tool Poisoning in realistic MCP settings. MCPTox is constructed upon 45 live, real-world MCP servers and 353 authentic tools. To achieve this, we design three distinct attack templates to generate a comprehensive suite of 1312 malicious test cases by few-shot learning, covering 10 categories of potential risks. Our evaluation on 20 prominent LLM agents setting reveals a widespread vulnerability to Tool Poisoning, with o1-mini, achieving an attack success rate of 72.8\%. We find that more capable models are often more susceptible, as the attack exploits their superior instruction-following abilities. Finally, the failure case analysis reveals that agents rarely refuse these attacks, with the highest refused rate (Claude-3.7-Sonnet) less than 3\%, demonstrating that existing safety alignment is ineffective against malicious actions that use legitimate tools for unauthorized operation. Our findings create a crucial empirical baseline for understanding and mitigating this widespread threat, and we release MCPTox for the development of verifiably safer AI agents. Our dataset is available at an anonymized repository: \textit{this https URL}.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Tool Poisoning is a distinct attack vector from Indirect Prompt Injection, as IPI payloads adapted for TPA achieved nearly 0% success, necessitating specialized benchmarks and defense strategies.
More capable LLM agents and those with enabled reasoning modes are more susceptible to Tool Poisoning, exhibiting an 'Inverse Scaling' phenomenon for this attack type.
Current content-based safety alignment mechanisms in LLMs are largely ineffective against Tool Poisoning, with refusal rates observed to be less than 3%.
originalProblem: LLM agents interacting with external tools, streamlined by protocols like MCP, introduce new and unquantified attack surfaces.
Prior LLM security benchmarks primarily focus on 'Indirect Prompt Injection' (IPI) attacks that manipulate tool execution results, often in simulated environments.

A significant gap existed in systematically evaluating 'Tool Poisoning Attacks' (TPA), where malicious instructions are embedded directly in a tool's metadata, affecting agents at the pre-execution reasoning stage on real-world systems.
results: A widespread vulnerability to Tool Poisoning was observed across 20 diverse LLM agents, with an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 36.5%; o1-mini (72.8% ASR) and Phi-4 (70.2% ASR) were among the most vulnerable models.

The 'Implicit Trigger - Parameter Tampering' paradigm was the most effective attack type, achieving an average ASR of 46.7%, indicating agents' susceptibility to subtle modifications of legitimate tool parameters.
Enabling reasoning mode in models like Qwen3 significantly increased their vulnerability, leading to an average ASR increase of 27.8%.
solution: Created MCPTox, a public benchmark for Tool Poisoning Attacks on real-world MCP servers, incorporating 45 live servers, 353 authentic tools, and 1312 malicious test cases across 10 risk categories.
Defined three distinct attack paradigms (Explicit/Implicit Trigger - Function Hijacking, Implicit Trigger - Parameter Tampering) to comprehensively test different methods of compromising LLM agents.

Developed a semi-automated test case generation process using LLMs to craft benign user queries and poisoned tool descriptions, followed by manual verification, ensuring the malicious action is performed by a legitimate server tool.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Science and Technology of China and Beihang University developed MCPTox, the first benchmark for Tool Poisoning Attacks on real-world Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Their evaluation revealed widespread vulnerability across 20 state-of-the-art LLM agents, with an average attack success rate of 36.5%, and demonstrated that existing Indirect Prompt Injection payloads are largely ineffective against this new attack vector.

title: MCPTox: A Benchmark for Tool Poisoning Attack on Real-World MCP Servers

abstract: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw run with elevated privileges on users' local machines, where a single successful prompt injection can leak credentials, redirect financial transactions, or destroy files. This threat goes well beyond conventional text-level jailbreaks, yet existing safety evaluations fall short: most test models in isolated chat settings, rely on synthetic environments, and do not account for how the agent framework itself shapes safety outcomes. We introduce CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios organized along three dimensions (harm domain, attack vector, and harmful action type) and grounded in realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces spanning software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, and DevOps. Each test case embeds adversarial content in one of three channels the agent encounters during normal work: workspace skill files, emails from trusted senders, and web pages. We evaluate five frontier LLMs as agent backbones, running 2,520 sandboxed trials across all configurations.
