Attack success rates (ASR) range from 40\% to 75\% across models and vary sharply by injection vector, with skill instructions (highest trust) consistently more dangerous than email or web content. Action-trace analysis reveals that the strongest model maintains hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions, while weaker models permit both. Cross-scaffold experiments on three agent frameworks further demonstrate that safety is not determined by the backbone model alone but depends on the full deployment stack, calling for safety evaluation that treats model and framework as joint variables. Code and data will be available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Chat-level safety of LLMs does not reliably translate to action-level safety in autonomous agents; models safe in chat can still perform harmful actions via tool calls.
Agent safety is a system-level property influenced not only by the backbone LLM but also significantly by the agent framework's scaffolding, tool orchestration, and memory management.

Attack vectors exhibit a trust-level gradient (skill > email > web injection), with agent defenses often being intent-sensitive (e.g., differentiating imperative vs. declarative phrasing) rather than purely content-sensitive.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents powered by LLMs operate with elevated privileges on user machines, creating a larger attack surface susceptible to indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks that can cause real-world harm.

Prior safety research primarily focused on text-level vulnerabilities and isolated chat environments, failing to quantify the compliance gap between chat-level safety and agent-level action execution.
Existing benchmarks often rely on synthetic environments and do not jointly consider the LLM backbone and agent framework as co-variables, obscuring their combined influence on adversarial risk.
results: Frontier LLMs exhibited overall Attack Success Rates (ASR) ranging from 40% to 75% against indirect prompt injections, with Claude Sonnet 4.6 demonstrating the lowest ASR (40.0%) and GPT-5.1 the highest (75.0%).
A consistent trust-level gradient was observed across injection vectors on OpenClaw, with skill injection achieving the highest ASR (69.4%) and web injection the lowest (38.4%).

Agent scaffold choice alone shifted Claude Sonnet 4.6's overall ASR by 8.6 percentage points (from 40.0% to 48.6%), and reversed the trust-level gradient for email vs. skill injection on different frameworks.
solution: Introduced CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark designed to evaluate personal AI agents against realistic indirect prompt injection threats in high-privilege professional workspaces.
Developed a multi-dimensional threat taxonomy categorizing adversarial scenarios by harm domain, attack vector (skill, email, web injection), and harmful action type.
Employed realistic workspace construction, multi-turn conversation design, and iterative refinement of attack vectors to simulate authentic adversarial conditions.
paper_summary.summary: The CLAWSAFETY benchmark assesses the safety of personal AI agents against indirect prompt injection, revealing that frontier LLMs exhibit 40-75% attack success rates in agentic environments despite chat-level safety. The study shows agent framework choice and attack vector trust levels modulate these vulnerabilities.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are evolving into agentic systems that reason, plan, and operate external tools. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a key enabler of this transition, offering a standardized interface for connecting LLMs with heterogeneous tools and services. Yet MCP's openness and multi-server workflows introduce new safety risks that existing benchmarks fail to capture, as they focus on isolated attacks or lack real-world coverage. We present MCP-SafetyBench, a comprehensive benchmark built on real MCP servers that supports realistic multi-turn evaluation across five domains: browser automation, financial analysis, location navigation, repository management, and web search. It incorporates a unified taxonomy of 20 MCP attack types spanning server, host, and user sides, and includes tasks requiring multi-step reasoning and cross-server coordination under uncertainty. Using MCP-SafetyBench, we systematically evaluate leading open- and closed-source LLMs, revealing that all models remain vulnerable to MCP attacks, with a notable safety-utility trade-off. Our results highlight the urgent need for stronger defenses and establish MCP-SafetyBench as a foundation for diagnosing and mitigating safety risks in real-world MCP deployments.
title: MCP-SafetyBench: A Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Large Language Models with Real-World MCP Servers

abstract: AI agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are being deployed at scale, yet we lack a systematic understanding of how the choice of backbone LLM affects agent security. The non-deterministic sequential nature of AI agents complicates security modeling, while the integration of traditional software with AI components entangles novel LLM vulnerabilities with conventional security risks. Existing frameworks only partially address these challenges as they either capture specific vulnerabilities only or require modeling of complete agents. To address these limitations, we introduce threat snapshots: a framework that isolates specific states in an agent's execution flow where LLM vulnerabilities manifest, enabling the systematic identification and categorization of security risks that propagate from the LLM to the agent level. We apply this framework to construct the $b^3$ benchmark, a security benchmark based on 194,331 unique crowdsourced adversarial attacks. We then evaluate 34 popular LLMs with it, revealing, among other insights, that enhanced reasoning capabilities improve security, while model size does not correlate with security. We release our benchmark, dataset, and evaluation code to facilitate widespread adoption by LLM providers and practitioners, offering guidance for agent developers and incentivizing model developers to prioritize backbone security improvements.

keyInsights: The choice of backbone LLM significantly impacts an AI agent's overall security posture.
An LLM's security properties are not uniform; performance varies considerably when evaluated across different agentic task types, indicating use-case specific security considerations.
While generally more capable models tend to be more secure, high capability alone does not guarantee superior security, as some top-capability models did not rank highest in security.
originalProblem: AI agents, powered by non-deterministic and black-box LLMs, introduce new security challenges not fully addressed by existing frameworks.
LLMs' inability to programmatically differentiate between data and instructions creates novel vulnerabilities that can become entangled with traditional software risks when agents use tools.

Current security evaluation frameworks for AI agents are limited, often focusing on narrow vulnerability types, requiring complex modeling of entire agent execution flows, or prioritizing broad safety over specific security exploitation.
results: The `claude-haiku-4-5` and `claude-sonnet-4-5` models (with reasoning enabled) were identified as the most secure among the 34 evaluated LLMs.

Enabling reasoning capabilities generally correlated with improved security for most models, though smaller models like `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` and `gpt-5-nano` showed decreased security with reasoning.

Closed-weight LLM systems generally demonstrated superior security compared to open-weight models, although the top open-weight model (`kimi-k2-thinking`) surpassed some frontier closed-weight models.
solution: Developed "threat snapshots," a systematic framework to abstractly capture and evaluate LLM vulnerabilities by isolating specific states within an agent's execution flow.
Created a comprehensive attack categorization for AI agent security, considering both attack vectors and objectives, as well as specific LLM task types affected.
Constructed the "b3 benchmark" by integrating the threat snapshots with a dataset of 210 high-quality, crowdsourced adversarial attacks designed across three difficulty levels.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces the "threat snapshots" framework and the "b3 benchmark" to systematically evaluate the security of backbone large language models (LLMs) within AI agents. The evaluation of 34 popular LLMs revealed that enabling reasoning generally improves security, closed-weight models often outperform open-weight models, and an LLM's security performance can vary significantly across different agentic task types.

title: Breaking Agent Backbones: Evaluating the Security of Backbone LLMs in AI Agents

abstract:
Recently, autonomous agents built on large language models (LLMs) have
experienced significant development and are being deployed in real-world
applications. These agents can extend the base LLM's capabilities in multiple
ways. For example, a well-built agent using GPT-3.5-Turbo as its core can
outperform the more advanced GPT-4 model by leveraging external components.
More importantly, the usage of tools enables these systems to perform actions
in the real world, moving from merely generating text to actively interacting
with their environment. Given the agents' practical applications and their
ability to execute consequential actions, it is crucial to assess potential
vulnerabilities. Such autonomous systems can cause more severe damage than a
standalone language model if compromised. While some existing research has
explored harmful actions by LLM agents, our study approaches the vulnerability
from a different perspective. We introduce a new type of attack that causes
malfunctions by misleading the agent into executing repetitive or irrelevant
actions. We conduct comprehensive evaluations using various attack methods,
surfaces, and properties to pinpoint areas of susceptibility. Our experiments
reveal that these attacks can induce failure rates exceeding 80\% in multiple
scenarios. Through attacks on implemented and deployable agents in multi-agent
scenarios, we accentuate the realistic risks associated with these
vulnerabilities. To mitigate such attacks, we propose self-examination
detection methods. However, our findings indicate these attacks are difficult
to detect effectively using LLMs alone, highlighting the substantial risks
associated with this vulnerability.

organization_info.name: CISPA – Helmholtz Center for Information Security

keyInsights: Malfunction amplification represents a distinct and effective class of attack that compromises LLM agents by causing systemic disruption rather than overt, policy-violating harm.
Prompt injection is the most potent attack method, demonstrating high success rates across various LLMs and agent types by overriding instructions.
Current LLM-based self-examination defenses are largely ineffective against malfunction attacks, failing to detect them compared to their high detection rates for overtly harmful prompts.
originalProblem: Existing LLM security research primarily focused on "red-teaming" techniques to elicit overtly harmful or policy-violating content from models.
The risk of subtle malfunctions in autonomous LLM agents, which can perform real-world actions, was largely overlooked compared to direct, malicious outputs.
Autonomous LLM agents possess inherent instability, making them susceptible to failures from reasoning errors or random responses that could be intentionally exacerbated.
results: Infinite loop attacks achieved a 59.4% Attack Success Rate (ASR) on emulated GPT-3.5-Turbo agents, nearly quadrupling the baseline failure rate, and reached 88.1% ASR on Claude-2 via prompt injection.
Multi-agent scenarios demonstrated viral propagation, with attacks spreading from robust to susceptible agents, leading to 75-80% ASR in various-type multi-agent settings.
Existing policy violation detection mechanisms, while 90-100% effective against traditional harmful prompts, showed 0% detection for infinite loop or incorrect function malfunction attacks.
solution: Proposed "malfunction amplification" attacks categorized into infinite loops (trapping agents in repetition) and incorrect function execution (triggering irrelevant actions).
Implemented attack methodologies including prompt injection, adversarial perturbation (e.g., GCG, SCPN), and adversarial demonstration, targeting agent inputs, intermediate outputs, and memory.
Evaluated attacks using a large-scale agent emulator and two fully implemented agents (Gmail and CSV agents) built with LangChain, assessing vulnerability and defense efficacy.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CISPA Helmholtz Center and collaborators introduced "malfunction amplification" attacks against autonomous LLM agents, demonstrating how subtle manipulations can induce infinite loops or irrelevant actions, rendering agents ineffective. These attacks achieved high success rates, up to 88.1% on Claude-2, and bypassed existing LLM-based safety defenses designed to detect overtly harmful content.

title:
Breaking Agents: Compromising Autonomous LLM Agents Through Malfunction
  Amplification

abstract:
AI agent frameworks connecting large language model (LLM) reasoning to host execution surfaces--shell, filesystem, containers, and messaging--introduce security challenges structurally distinct from conventional software. We present a systematic taxonomy of 190 advisories filed against OpenClaw, an open-source AI agent runtime, organized by architectural layer and trust-violation type. Vulnerabilities cluster along two orthogonal axes: (1) the system axis, reflecting the architectural layer (exec policy, gateway, channel, sandbox, browser, plugin, agent/prompt); and (2) the attack axis, reflecting adversarial techniques (identity spoofing, policy bypass, cross-layer composition, prompt injection, supply-chain escalation).
Patch-differential evidence yields three principal findings. First, three Moderate- or High-severity advisories in the Gateway and Node-Host subsystems compose into a complete unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) path--spanning delivery, exploitation, and command-and-control--from an LLM tool call to the host process. Second, the exec allowlist, the primary command-filtering mechanism, relies on a closed-world assumption that command identity is recoverable via lexical parsing. This is invalidated by shell line continuation, busybox multiplexing, and GNU option abbreviation. Third, a malicious skill distributed via the plugin channel executed a two-stage dropper within the LLM context, bypassing the exec pipeline and demonstrating that the skill distribution surface lacks runtime policy enforcement. The dominant structural weakness is per-layer trust enforcement rather than unified policy boundaries, making cross-layer attacks resilient to local remediation.

keyInsights: Securing autonomous AI agent frameworks necessitates a shift from conventional software security paradigms, recognizing the LLM's output as a primary control signal and a distinct attack surface.

Systemic architectural weaknesses, such as decentralized trust enforcement and brittle lexical assumptions in policy engines, are more prevalent than isolated defects and enable complex exploitation chains.
Adversaries can corrupt an LLM's reasoning context (Context Manipulation) to induce malicious actions, representing a unique threat model that operates above traditional enforcement layers.
originalProblem: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into autonomous AI agent frameworks introduces a new class of security challenges, as LLM outputs act as control signals for real-world actions.
Prior research often characterizes individual attack techniques without a unifying framework to map vulnerabilities to specific architectural layers.

The rapid proliferation and adoption of frameworks like OpenClaw, accompanied by numerous disclosed security advisories, highlighted an urgent need for a systematic understanding of their security landscape.
results: Identified three principal findings: chained unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) across Gateway and Node-Host subsystems, systematic bypasses of the `exec` allowlist due to flawed lexical parsing, and malicious skill distribution that bypassed runtime policies.

The Exec Policy Engine accounted for the most advisories (46), while the Gateway WebSocket Interface had the highest count of High-severity findings, and the Container Boundary included the only Critical-severity finding.
The dominant structural pattern identified was a reliance on per-layer, per-call-site trust enforcement, which made cross-layer composition attacks resistant to localized remediation efforts.
solution: A two-axis security taxonomy was developed, classifying vulnerabilities by their architectural location (system axis with 10 layers) and the adversarial technique employed (attack axis, adapting the Cyber Kill Chain).
This taxonomy was applied to a corpus of 190 security advisories against the OpenClaw framework, using patch-differential evidence to identify architectural root causes.
The Cyber Kill Chain was extended with a novel stage, "Context Manipulation," to specifically account for adversarial influence on the LLM's reasoning layer as an attack surface.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Texas A&M University developed a systematic security taxonomy for AI agent frameworks by analyzing 190 advisories against the OpenClaw framework. This work identifies systemic architectural weaknesses and introduces "Context Manipulation" as a novel stage in the AI agent attack kill chain, providing a structured approach for threat modeling.

title: A Systematic Taxonomy of Security Vulnerabilities in the OpenClaw AI Agent Framework

abstract: Autonomous coding agents are increasingly integrated into software development workflows, offering capabilities that extend beyond code suggestion to active system interaction and environment management. OpenClaw, a representative platform in this emerging paradigm, introduces an extensible skill ecosystem that allows third-party developers to inject behavioral guidance through lifecycle hooks during agent initialization. While this design enhances automation and customization, it also opens a novel and unexplored attack surface. In this paper, we identify and systematically characterize guidance injection, a stealthy attack vector that embeds adversarial operational narratives into bootstrap guidance files. Unlike traditional prompt injection, which relies on explicit malicious instructions, guidance injection manipulates the agent's reasoning context by framing harmful actions as routine best practices. These narratives are automatically incorporated into the agent's interpretive framework and influence future task execution without raising this http URL construct 26 malicious skills spanning 13 attack categories including credential exfiltration, workspace destruction, privilege escalation, and persistent backdoor installation. We evaluate them using ORE-Bench, a realistic developer workspace benchmark we developed. Across 52 natural user prompts and six state-of-the-art LLM backends, our attacks achieve success rates from 16.0% to 64.2%, with the majority of malicious actions executed autonomously without user confirmation. Furthermore, 94% of our malicious skills evade detection by existing static and LLM-based scanners. Our findings reveal fundamental tensions in the design of autonomous agent ecosystems and underscore the urgent need for defenses based on capability isolation, runtime policy enforcement, and transparent guidance provenance.

keyInsights: Guidance injection enables cognitive-level manipulation, where attackers redefine an agent's perception of "normal" or "best practice" behavior through trusted operational narratives, making it fundamentally different from overt command injection.

The attack exploits architectural features like lifecycle hooks and bootstrap context, demonstrating that mechanisms intended for extensibility and customization can introduce critical vulnerabilities by shaping an agent's foundational reasoning.

Existing static analysis, general LLM-based input scanners (e.g., LLM-Guard), and commercial skill vetting platforms are largely ineffective, failing to detect 94% of malicious skills due to the subtle and diffused nature of embedded malicious intent.
originalProblem: Autonomous coding agents, increasingly integrated into software development, are exposed to known vulnerabilities like prompt and skill injection, but new, subtler forms of reasoning manipulation were largely unexplored.

OpenClaw's design allows third-party skills to inject "bootstrap guidance" during agent initialization, providing a mechanism for agents to access private data and execute system-level operations based on potentially untrusted information.
The inherent trust placed in these guidance files, coupled with LLM properties, presented an unaddressed security risk distinct from explicit command injection.
results: Guidance injection successfully induced autonomous agents to perform unauthorized actions across high, medium, and low-risk categories, with DeepSeek V3.2 exhibiting the highest vulnerability and Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.6 showing more resistance.
Supply Chain attacks achieved the highest overall success rates, exploiting models' susceptibility to recommendations framed as "optimization" or "best practices."

Case studies demonstrated practical impacts, including the deletion of `.git` directories, exfiltration of SSH keys and cloud credentials, setup of MITM proxies for privacy invasion, and injection of promotional content into agent responses.
solution: Researchers analyzed OpenClaw's skill execution pipeline, identifying the `agent:bootstrap` hook and `context.bootstrapFiles` as the injection surface for adversarial operational narratives.

They designed a "guidance injection" attack mechanism leveraging LLM properties like the primacy effect and associative inference, employing strategies such as Authority Implantation and Distributed Concealment.

A dual-role LLM-based generation process created 26 malicious skills across 13 categories, which were evaluated in a realistic simulated developer environment (ORE-Bench) against six state-of-the-art LLM backbones using ambiguous trigger prompts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Shanghai Jiao Tong University identified guidance injection, a distinct attack vector that manipulates autonomous coding agents like OpenClaw by subtly influencing their foundational reasoning through injected "bootstrap guidance" files. This cognitive-level manipulation led agents to perform unauthorized actions across various risk categories, with 94% of malicious skills evading detection by current static and LLM-based security tools.

title: Trojan's Whisper: Stealthy Manipulation of OpenClaw through Injected Bootstrapped Guidance

abstract: Computer-use agent (CUA) frameworks, powered by large language models (LLMs) or multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), are rapidly maturing as assistants that can perceive context, reason, and act directly within software environments. Among their most critical applications is operating system (OS) control. As CUAs in the OS domain become increasingly embedded in daily operations, it is imperative to examine their real-world security implications, specifically whether CUAs can be misused to perform realistic, security-relevant attacks. Existing works exhibit four major limitations: Missing attacker-knowledge model on tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTP), Incomplete coverage for end-to-end kill chains, unrealistic environment without multi-host and encrypted user credentials, and unreliable judgment dependent on LLM-as-a-Judge. To address these gaps, we propose AdvCUA, the first benchmark aligned with real-world TTPs in MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix, which comprises 140 tasks, including 40 direct malicious tasks, 74 TTP-based malicious tasks, and 26 end-to-end kill chains, systematically evaluates CUAs under a realistic enterprise OS security threat in a multi-host environment sandbox by hard-coded evaluation. We evaluate the existing five mainstream CUAs, including ReAct, AutoGPT, Gemini CLI, Cursor CLI, and Cursor IDE based on 8 foundation LLMs. The results demonstrate that current frontier CUAs do not adequately cover OS security-centric threats. These capabilities of CUAs reduce dependence on custom malware and deep domain expertise, enabling even inexperienced attackers to mount complex enterprise intrusions, which raises social concern about the responsibility and security of CUAs.

keyInsights: Frontier CUAs exhibit a significant gap in current safety alignments, demonstrating a willingness to attempt malicious tasks and successfully execute complex attacks.
Jailbreak attacks can dramatically amplify real-world threats, leading to substantially higher success rates for complex end-to-end kill chains in certain CUA configurations.
Specific MITRE ATT&CK tactics, particularly Defense Evasion, Persistence, and Privilege Escalation, are highly vulnerable to CUA exploitation, requiring focused safety hardening.
originalProblem: Existing CUA security benchmarks lack comprehensive attacker-knowledge models (TTPs) and detailed end-to-end attack kill chain coverage.
Current evaluations often occur in unrealistic single-host environments with plaintext secrets, failing to mirror multi-host enterprise setups with encrypted credentials.
Many benchmarks rely on 'LLM-as-a-Judge' for evaluation, leading to inconsistent and unreliable assessments of CUA malicious behaviors.
results: Cursor CLI (Claude Opus 4.1) achieved the highest ASR@5 of 69.59% on TTP-based malicious tasks.
ReAct-based GPT-4.1 achieved 50% ASR@5 on end-to-end kill chains, which increased to 96.51% ASR@5 when a jailbreak was applied.

The Bypass Success Rate (BSR) exceeded the Attack Success Rate (ASR) by an average of 24.17% across all task types, indicating that CUAs often exhibit malicious intent without fully completing the task.
Existing defense mechanisms like LLaMA Guard 4, OpenAI Moderation API, and prompt-based self-reminders showed limited effectiveness against TTP-based malicious requests.
solution: Developed AdvCUA, a benchmark grounded in the MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix, comprising 140 tasks including direct malicious, TTP-based, and end-to-end kill chains.
Configured a Docker-based corporate microsandbox to simulate a multi-host enterprise OS environment with realistic elements like SSH/SCP and distributed, encrypted credentials.
Employed Bypass Success Rate (BSR@n) and Attack Success Rate (ASR@n) metrics, verified by four hard-coded and deterministic protocols (Match, Trigger, Probe, Verify).
paper_summary.summary: A multi-institutional research effort, including Apple, introduced AdvCUA, a benchmark evaluating the real-world cyber offensive capabilities of Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) against enterprise operating systems. The study demonstrated that frontier CUAs achieved average Attack Success Rates (ASR@5) up to 69.59% on TTP-based malicious tasks and could execute end-to-end kill chains, with some configurations reaching 96.51% ASR@5 with jailbreaks.

title: Code Agent can be an End-to-end System Hacker: Benchmarking Real-world Threats of Computer-use Agent

abstract: Computer-use agents (CUAs) promise to automate complex tasks across operating systems (OS) and the web, but remain vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. Current evaluations of this threat either lack support realistic but controlled environments or ignore hybrid web-OS attack scenarios involving both interfaces. To address this, we propose RedTeamCUA, an adversarial testing framework featuring a novel hybrid sandbox that integrates a VM-based OS environment with Docker-based web platforms. Our sandbox supports key features tailored for red teaming, such as flexible adversarial scenario configuration, and a setting that decouples adversarial evaluation from navigational limitations of CUAs by initializing tests directly at the point of an adversarial injection. Using RedTeamCUA, we develop RTC-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark with 864 examples that investigate realistic, hybrid web-OS attack scenarios and fundamental security vulnerabilities. Benchmarking current frontier CUAs identifies significant vulnerabilities: Claude 3.7 Sonnet | CUA demonstrates an ASR of 42.9%, while Operator, the most secure CUA evaluated, still exhibits an ASR of 7.6%. Notably, CUAs often attempt to execute adversarial tasks with an Attempt Rate as high as 92.5%, although failing to complete them due to capability limitations. Nevertheless, we observe concerning high ASRs in realistic end-to-end settings, with the strongest-to-date Claude 4.5 Sonnet | CUA exhibiting the highest ASR of 60%, indicating that CUA threats can already result in tangible risks to users and computer systems. Overall, RedTeamCUA provides an essential framework for advancing realistic, controlled, and systematic analysis of CUA vulnerabilities, highlighting the urgent need for robust defenses to indirect prompt injection prior to real-world deployment.
title: RedTeamCUA: Realistic Adversarial Testing of Computer-Use Agents in Hybrid Web-OS Environments

abstract: Although large language model (LLM)-based agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, are increasingly evolving from task-oriented systems into personalized AI assistants for solving complex real-world tasks, their practical deployment also introduces severe security risks. However, existing agent security research and evaluation frameworks primarily focus on synthetic or task-centric settings, and thus fail to accurately capture the attack surface and risk propagation mechanisms of personalized agents in real-world deployments. To address this gap, we propose Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework tailored for real-world personalized agents. Building upon existing agent attack paradigms, PASB incorporates personalized usage scenarios, realistic toolchains, and long-horizon interactions, enabling black-box, end-to-end security evaluation on real systems. Using OpenClaw as a representative case study, we systematically evaluate its security across multiple personalized scenarios, tool capabilities, and attack types. Our results indicate that OpenClaw exhibits critical vulnerabilities at different execution stages, including user prompt processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval, highlighting substantial security risks in personalized agent deployments. The code for the proposed PASB framework is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Data Science & Artificial Intelligence Research Institute, China Unicom

keyInsights: Vulnerabilities in personalized LLM agents are not isolated, but instead propagate across different execution stages (prompt processing, tool invocation, memory retrieval) and accumulate over long-horizon interactions.
Current prompt-level defenses (e.g., Delimiter, Sandwich) and instruction-based memory protections are insufficient, as residual attack success rates persist even with these applied safeguards.

Effective security evaluation for personalized agents requires a shift from text-level analysis to action-chain and system-level assessment to accurately capture risks in deployed, continuously operating AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing security evaluation frameworks for LLM agents are inadequate for real-world personalized deployments, being primarily tailored for synthetic or task-centric settings.
Personalized AI agents, characterized by persistent operation, accumulation of private user context, and access to high-privilege tools, introduce severe and unaddressed security risks.
Security failures in this new paradigm can lead to tangible harms like unsafe actions and private asset exfiltration, necessitating a comprehensive, system-level security assessment.
results: OpenClaw demonstrated pervasive vulnerabilities, with indirect prompt injection and tool-return attacks achieving Attack Success Rates (ASRs) up to 66.8% without defenses.
Despite the application of prompt-layer defenses, a non-trivial residual ASR persisted across all models and attack types, ranging from 10.5% to 22.0%, indicating continued exposure.

Long-Term Memory extraction and modification were feasible (e.g., 62.5% LTM extraction, 71.5% LTM modification for Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct without defenses), highlighting significant memory-related risks.
solution: The research proposes Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework specifically designed for real-world personalized LLM agents.

PASB formalizes attack tasks by focusing on observable execution traces, including tool calls and their arguments, and defines attack success based on system-level harm predicates like information leakage, unsafe actions, and persistent behaviors.

The framework enables black-box, end-to-end security evaluation in realistic personalized scenarios (external content, memory management, tool interaction risks) using deployed systems such as OpenClaw.
paper_summary.summary: Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB) is introduced as a framework for evaluating the security of real-world, personalized Large Language Model (LLM) agents. Its evaluation of OpenClaw, a representative personalized agent, found pervasive vulnerabilities where attacks propagated across execution stages and persisted over long interactions, leading to unauthorized actions and data exfiltration, even when existing defenses were applied.

title: From Assistant to Double Agent: Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks on OpenClaw for Personalized Local AI Agent

abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed in long-horizon, complex environments to solve challenging problems, but this expansion exposes them to long-horizon attacks that exploit multi-turn user-agent-environment interactions to achieve objectives infeasible in single-turn settings. To measure agent vulnerabilities to such risks, we present AgentLAB, the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating LLM agent susceptibility to adaptive, long-horizon attacks. Currently, AgentLAB supports five novel attack types including intent hijacking, tool chaining, task injection, objective drifting, and memory poisoning, spanning 28 realistic agentic environments, and 644 security test cases. Leveraging AgentLAB, we evaluate representative LLM agents and find that they remain highly susceptible to long-horizon attacks; moreover, defenses designed for single-turn interactions fail to reliably mitigate long-horizon threats. We anticipate that AgentLAB will serve as a valuable benchmark for tracking progress on securing LLM agents in practical settings. The benchmark is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM agents, even those powered by frontier models, possess fundamental and systemic vulnerabilities to long-horizon, adaptive attacks.

The temporal dimension and adaptivity of multi-turn interactions are critical factors that significantly enhance attack effectiveness, making long-horizon attacks much more potent than one-shot methods.

Current single-turn focused defense mechanisms are largely ineffective and inadequate against these complex, temporally extended threats, necessitating a paradigm shift towards tailored, context-aware defenses.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent security research and benchmarks predominantly focus on single-turn or static attacks, failing to capture realistic adversarial strategies.
Prior approaches neglect the temporal dimension where sophisticated adversaries exploit extended, multi-turn interactions over time.
This creates a critical gap in understanding and mitigating security risks for autonomous LLM agents deployed in complex, high-stakes environments.
results: Both open-weight and proprietary LLM agents, including GPT-5.1, showed high susceptibility to long-horizon attacks, with GPT-5.1 demonstrating an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of nearly 70%.
Long-horizon attacks significantly outperformed one-shot methods; for instance, task injection on GPT-4o saw ASR increase from 62.5% (one-shot) to 79.9% (long-horizon).
Ablation studies confirmed that ASR monotonically increases with the number of attack turns, and existing one-shot defenses failed to reliably mitigate these multi-turn threats.
solution: AgentLAB establishes the first benchmark specifically for evaluating LLM agent susceptibility to adaptive, long-horizon attacks, utilizing a modular framework with distinct components for agents, environments, tasks, and attacks.

A novel multi-agent framework is developed to implement five new long-horizon attack types (Intent Hijacking, Tool Chaining, Objective Drifting, Task Injection, Memory Poisoning) that exploit multi-turn interactions and agent capabilities.

The benchmark includes diverse realistic environments and test cases, using Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Turn to Success (T2S) as primary evaluation metrics, also assessing existing one-shot defenses.
paper_summary.summary: AgentLAB provides the first benchmark to assess Large Language Model (LLM) agent vulnerability to adaptive, multi-turn attacks, demonstrating that agents, including those based on frontier models, exhibit high susceptibility with GPT-5.1 reaching nearly 70% Attack Success Rate. The work shows these long-horizon attacks substantially outperform single-turn methods, revealing the ineffectiveness of current defenses.

title: AgentLAB: Benchmarking LLM Agents against Long-Horizon Attacks

abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents introduce security risks that extend beyond user-input filtering, including indirect prompt injection through fetched content, unsafe tool execution, credential leakage, and tampering with local control files. We present OpenClaw PRISM, a zero-fork runtime security layer for OpenClaw-based agent gateways. PRISM combines an in-process plugin with optional sidecar services and distributes enforcement across ten lifecycle hooks spanning message ingress, prompt construction, tool execution, tool-result persistence, outbound messaging, sub-agent spawning, and gateway startup. Rather than introducing a novel detection model, PRISM integrates a hybrid heuristic-plus-LLM scanning pipeline, conversation- and session-scoped risk accumulation with TTL-based decay, policy-enforced controls over tools, paths, private networks, domain tiers, and outbound secret patterns, and a tamper-evident audit and operations plane with integrity verification and hot-reloadable policy management. We outline an evaluation methodology and benchmark pipeline for measuring security effectiveness, false positives, layer contribution, runtime overhead, and operational recoverability in an agent-runtime setting, and we report current preliminary benchmark results on curated same-slice experiments and operational microbenchmarks. The system targets deployable runtime defense for real agent gateways rather than benchmark-only detection.

keyInsights: LLM agent security is fundamentally a systems problem requiring distributed, lifecycle-wide enforcement rather than isolated input/output filtering due to the expanded attack surface of tool-augmented agents.
A zero-fork, deployable architecture with operational features like hot-reloading and tamper-evident auditing is crucial for sustainable security in evolving agent frameworks.
Combining heuristic detection with LLM-assisted classification and stateful, graduated risk responses allows for comprehensive threat mitigation and proportional intervention.
originalProblem: Tool-augmented LLM agents expand the attack surface beyond traditional input filtering, enabling threats like prompt override, tool abuse, credential leakage, and control-file tampering across the entire runtime.

Existing defenses focusing on prompt classification or output filtering are insufficient for the multi-stage execution of tool-using agents, failing to address indirect injection or gradual risk accumulation.
Practical deployment of defenses requires zero-fork integration, maintainability, observability, and configurability, which current solutions often lack, creating operational security gaps.
results: The full PRISM configuration achieved a 95.5% attack blocking rate and a 13.9% false positive rate in a unified baseline-ladder test slice of 80 cases using a live local LLM.
The multi-layered design demonstrated a progressive increase in attack blocking and decrease in false positives as more PRISM layers were activated, confirming the benefit of scanner assistance.
Runtime overhead analysis indicated that local-model inference latency (up to 15.8 seconds for full PRISM p95) is the dominant performance cost, while memory delta remained low (below 1.4 MiB).
solution: OpenClaw PRISM implements a zero-fork runtime security layer for OpenClaw gateways, using an in-process plugin with optional sidecar services for distributed enforcement.
It integrates security controls across ten lifecycle hooks, employing a hybrid heuristic-first, LLM-assisted scanning pipeline and a time-decaying, thresholded risk accumulation mechanism.
The system provides unified policy-enforced governance over tool invocations, protected file paths, network access, and outbound Data Loss Prevention (DLP) checks.
paper_summary.summary: OpenClaw PRISM introduces a zero-fork, defense-in-depth runtime security layer for OpenClaw-based LLM agent gateways, distributing enforcement across the agent's lifecycle to counter diverse attack vectors. Preliminary evaluations show that the full PRISM configuration achieves 95.5% attack blocking while maintaining a 13.9% false positive rate in tested scenarios.

title: OpenClaw PRISM: A Zero-Fork, Defense-in-Depth Runtime Security Layer for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents

abstract: This work demonstrates that LLM-based web navigation agents offer powerful automation capabilities but are vulnerable to Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) attacks. We show that adversaries can embed universal adversarial triggers in webpage HTML to hijack agent behavior that utilizes the accessibility tree to parse HTML, causing unintended or malicious actions. Using the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) algorithm and a Browser Gym agent powered by Llama-3.1, our system demonstrates high success rates across real websites in both targeted and general attacks, including login credential exfiltration and forced ad clicks. Our empirical results highlight critical security risks and the need for stronger defenses as LLM-driven autonomous web agents become more widely adopted. The system software (this https URL) is released under the MIT License, with an accompanying publicly available demo website (this http URL).

keyInsights: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) via the HTML accessibility tree poses a practical and serious threat to LLM-based web navigation agents.

Universal adversarial triggers can be effectively optimized to hijack agent behavior across multiple user instructions on a single website, and even for specific malicious actions across different websites.
The attack method is robust, allowing successful execution of targeted actions even when the LLM's output includes minor syntactic variations.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM)-based web navigation agents are increasingly used for automation, creating new potential attack surfaces.
While Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) was a conceptual threat, its practical realization and empirical demonstration on complex, real-world LLM web navigation agents remained unclear.
LLM agents' reliance on parsing web content, such as the accessibility tree, for decision-making exposes them to external adversarial instructions embedded in webpages.
results: Achieved high attack success rates, with the lowest observed ASR of 0.83 for universal triggers on specific websites across diverse user instructions (Targeted Website Universal Instruction scenario).
Demonstrated cross-website transferability for credential exfiltration, successfully leaking at least one credential (username or password) for 6 out of 11 hold-out test login pages.

Found that adversarial triggers are largely model-specific, and triggers optimized for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct did not effectively transfer to other LLMs like Llama-2-7b-chat-hf or Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3.
solution: The researchers developed an attack by embedding universal adversarial triggers within the HTML of webpages, specifically leveraging how the LLM web agent (Browser Gym with Llama-3.1) processes the HTML accessibility tree.
They utilized a modified Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) algorithm to optimize these triggers, forcing the LLM to output pre-defined, attacker-desired actions overriding user instructions.

Experiments were conducted across three scenarios: targeted attacks for specific instructions on a single website, universal triggers for multiple instructions on a single website, and universal triggers for a specific malicious action across multiple distinct websites.
paper_summary.summary: This research empirically demonstrates indirect prompt injection attacks on Large Language Model-based web navigation agents by embedding adversarial triggers in webpage HTML's accessibility tree. The study shows these triggers can hijack agent behavior, leading to actions like data exfiltration or forced clicks, and quantifies high attack success rates across various realistic scenarios.

title: Manipulating LLM Web Agents with Indirect Prompt Injection Attack via HTML Accessibility Tree

abstract: Search agents connect LLMs to the Internet, enabling them to access broader and more up-to-date information. However, this also introduces a new threat surface: unreliable search results can mislead agents into producing unsafe outputs. Real-world incidents and our two in-the-wild observations show that such failures can occur in practice. To study this threat systematically, we propose SafeSearch, an automated red-teaming framework that is scalable, cost-efficient, and lightweight, enabling harmless safety evaluation of search agents. Using this, we generate 300 test cases spanning five risk categories (e.g., misinformation and prompt injection) and evaluate three search agent scaffolds across 17 representative LLMs. Our results reveal substantial vulnerabilities in LLM-based search agents, with the highest ASR reaching 90.5% for GPT-4.1-mini in a search-workflow setting. Moreover, we find that common defenses, such as reminder prompting, offer limited protection. Overall, SafeSearch provides a practical way to measure and improve the safety of LLM-based search agents. Our codebase and test cases are publicly available: this https URL.

keyInsights: Most LLM-based search agents exhibit high vulnerability to unreliable internet content, often uncritically incorporating deceptive information and generating unsafe responses.
Agent scaffold design plays a critical role in mitigating risks; more sophisticated deep-research scaffolds improve safety by enabling cross-validation and follow-up searches.
Susceptibility varies by risk type, with misinformation identified as the most severe threat to search agent safety, while indirect prompt injection showed comparatively lower vulnerability.
originalProblem: LLM-based search agents are vulnerable to unreliable content from external search tools, leading to potential unsafe or misleading outputs due to the unmoderated nature of the internet.
Existing safety evaluations for search agents primarily focus on task performance and factual accuracy, neglecting the specific threats posed by unreliable external search results.
Manual red-teaming for these agents is unscalable, costly, and raises ethical concerns due to the potential harm from real-world SEO manipulations.
results: Observed Attack Success Rates (ASR) reached up to 90.5% for agents like GPT-4.1-mini in the search workflow setting, demonstrating widespread vulnerability.
Advanced agent scaffolds substantially reduced ASRs for GPT-4.1-mini from 90.5% (search workflow) to 57.4% (deep-research scaffold), indicating improved resilience.
Baseline defense strategies, such as external filtering of search results, roughly halved ASRs but did not eliminate risks, while reminder prompting was largely ineffective.
solution: SAFESEARCH introduces an automated and systematic red-teaming framework that simulates unreliable search results by injecting LLM-generated deceptive websites into authentic search results.
The framework employs LLM assistants for automated generation of test cases, website content, quality filtering using differential testing, and checklist-assisted safety and helpfulness evaluations.
It covers five key risk types: indirect prompt injection, harmful outputs, bias-inducing content, sponsored advertisements, and misinformation, all stemming from unreliable search results.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed SAFESEARCH, an automated red-teaming framework designed to evaluate the safety of LLM-based search agents exposed to unreliable internet search results. The system systematically quantifies vulnerabilities across various risk types and agent architectures, revealing high susceptibility in current models and the critical role of agent scaffold design in improving safety.

title: SafeSearch: Automated Red-Teaming of LLM-Based Search Agents

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into real-world applications via the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a universal open standard for connecting AI agents with data sources and external tools. While MCP enhances the capabilities of LLM-based agents, it also introduces new security risks and significantly expands their attack surface. In this paper, we present the first formalization of a secure MCP and its required specifications. Based on this foundation, we establish a comprehensive MCP security taxonomy that extends existing models by incorporating protocol-level and host-side threats, identifying 17 distinct attack types across four primary attack surfaces. Building on these specifications, we introduce MCPSecBench, a systematic security benchmark and playground that integrates prompt datasets, MCP servers, MCP clients, attack scripts, a GUI test harness, and protection mechanisms to evaluate these threats across three major MCP platforms. MCPSecBench is designed to be modular and extensible, allowing researchers to incorporate custom implementations of clients, servers, and transport protocols for rigorous assessment. Our evaluation across three major MCP platforms reveals that all attack surfaces yield successful compromises. Core vulnerabilities universally affect Claude, OpenAI, and Cursor, while server-side and specific client-side attacks exhibit considerable variability across different hosts and models. Furthermore, current protection mechanisms proved largely ineffective, achieving an average success rate of less than 30%. Overall, MCPSecBench standardizes the evaluation of MCP security and enables rigorous testing across all protocol layers.
title: MCPSecBench: A Systematic Security Benchmark and Playground for Testing Model Context Protocols

abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an emerging standard designed to enable seamless interaction between Large Language Model (LLM) applications and external tools or resources. Within a short period, thousands of MCP services have been developed and deployed. However, the client-server integration architecture inherent in MCP may expand the attack surface against LLM Agent systems, introducing new vulnerabilities that allow attackers to exploit by designing malicious MCP servers. In this paper, we present the first end-to-end empirical evaluation of attack vectors targeting the MCP ecosystem. We identify four categories of attacks, i.e., Tool Poisoning Attacks, Puppet Attacks, Rug Pull Attacks, and Exploitation via Malicious External Resources. To evaluate their feasibility, we conduct experiments following the typical steps of launching an attack through malicious MCP servers: upload -> download -> attack. Specifically, we first construct malicious MCP servers and successfully upload them to three widely used MCP aggregation platforms. The results indicate that current audit mechanisms are insufficient to identify and prevent these threats. Next, through a user study and interview with 20 participants, we demonstrate that users struggle to identify malicious MCP servers and often unknowingly install them from aggregator platforms. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that these attacks can trigger harmful actions within the user's local environment, such as accessing private files or controlling devices to transfer digital assets. Additionally, based on interview results, we discuss four key challenges faced by the current MCP security ecosystem. These findings underscore the urgent need for robust security mechanisms to defend against malicious MCP servers and ensure the safe deployment of increasingly autonomous LLM agents.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: MCP aggregation platforms function as directories without robust, continuous audit mechanisms, failing to detect malicious server uploads or post-approval 'rug pull' attacks.
Users, even those with technical backgrounds, demonstrate low security awareness and struggle to identify malicious MCP servers and their underlying attack vectors.
Mainstream LLMs and MCP clients inherently trust tool outputs and lack sufficient built-in defenses against malicious prompts embedded within tool descriptions, making them highly vulnerable.
originalProblem: The rapidly expanding Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, which facilitates LLM agent interaction with external tools, lacked comprehensive security evaluation.

Existing research in MCP security primarily focused on traditional server vulnerabilities or theoretical defenses, without empirical end-to-end validation of attack vectors within the unique client-server trust boundaries.
The modularity of MCP introduces new attack surfaces as LLM clients interact with potentially untrusted external MCP servers.
results: All three tested MCP aggregation platforms (Smithery.ai, MCP.so, Glama) accepted malicious servers without detection, and 'rug pull' attacks were successfully demonstrated by modifying approved repositories.
In the user study, 75% of participants inadvertently selected at least one malicious server, and only 5% correctly identified all malicious servers even when informed of their presence.

Attack vectors like 'Exploitation via Malicious External Resources' achieved an average success rate of 93.33%, with LLM refusal rates mostly below 10%, demonstrating high vulnerability to privacy theft, result manipulation, and cryptocurrency theft across various LLMs and clients.
solution: Conducted the first end-to-end empirical evaluation of attack vectors in the MCP ecosystem, simulating the complete lifecycle from malicious server upload to local environment exploitation.
Developed and tested malicious MCP servers against three major aggregation platforms, five mainstream LLMs (e.g., Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4o), and five common MCP clients.
Performed a user study with 20 participants to assess their ability to identify malicious servers and their general security awareness regarding LLM agent systems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers conducted the first end-to-end empirical evaluation of security vulnerabilities in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, which allows Large Language Model (LLM) agents to interact with external tools. The study revealed that current MCP aggregation platforms lack audit mechanisms, users exhibit low security awareness, and mainstream LLMs and clients are highly susceptible to malicious MCP server attacks, with some attack vectors achieving over 90% success rates in tasks like privacy theft and result manipulation.

title: Beyond the Protocol: Unveiling Attack Vectors in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) Ecosystem

abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) into autonomous, tool-calling agents has fundamentally altered the cybersecurity landscape. Frameworks like OpenClaw grant AI systems operating-system-level permissions and the autonomy to execute complex workflows. This level of access creates unprecedented security challenges. Consequently, traditional content-filtering defenses have become obsolete. This report presents a comprehensive security analysis of the OpenClaw ecosystem. We systematically investigate its current threat landscape, highlighting critical vulnerabilities such as prompt injection-driven Remote Code Execution (RCE), sequential tool attack chains, context amnesia, and supply chain contamination. To systematically contextualize these threats, we propose a novel tri-layered risk taxonomy for autonomous Agents, categorizing vulnerabilities across AI Cognitive, Software Execution, and Information System dimensions. To address these systemic architectural flaws, we introduce the Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA). This theoretical defense blueprint advocates for zero-trust agentic execution, dynamic intent verification, and cross-layer reasoning-action correlation. Building on this framework, we present Project ClawGuard, our ongoing engineering initiative. This project aims to implement the FASA paradigm and transition autonomous agents from high-risk experimental utilities into trustworthy systems. Our code and dataset are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The ability of autonomous agents to directly interact with operating systems and external tools fundamentally redefines AI security boundaries, requiring system-level rather than just content-level defenses.

Vulnerabilities like prompt injection are amplified in autonomous agents, transitioning from altering chat responses to enabling critical system-level threats such as Remote Code Execution (RCE) and data exfiltration.

Cognitive aspects like context window compression can lead to "instruction amnesia," causing agents to disregard safety constraints, while Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are susceptible to "memory pollution" for persistent backdoors.
originalProblem: Autonomous, tool-calling AI agents, exemplified by frameworks like OpenClaw, are granted operating-system-level permissions, extending security boundaries beyond traditional LLMs.
Existing LLM security research primarily addresses content-centric risks, failing to account for system-level threats such as Remote Code Execution (RCE) or data exfiltration through agent actions.

Conventional security defenses (e.g., WAFs, input filtering) are insufficient against autonomous agents due to their instruction-data conflation and ability to chain legitimate tools for malicious objectives.
results: Identified specific critical vulnerabilities in OpenClaw, including prompt injection, context amnesia leading to catastrophic failures, memory pollution creating soft backdoors, sandbox isolation failures, and chained tool execution for data exfiltration.
Developed a Tri-layered Risk Taxonomy that systematically categorizes autonomous agent vulnerabilities across AI Cognitive, Software Execution, and Information System dimensions.

Designed the Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA), a four-layered defense blueprint advocating for zero-trust agentic execution, dynamic intent verification, and cross-layer reasoning-action correlation to secure agents from input to OS-level execution.
solution: Conducted a detailed architectural analysis of the OpenClaw framework, a popular open-source autonomous agent, to identify potential attack surfaces.

Empirically investigated and detailed various security threats, including cognitive manipulation (prompt injection, context amnesia), software execution flaws (sandbox isolation failures, tool hijacking), and information system vulnerabilities (insecure state storage).
Proposed a novel Tri-layered Risk Taxonomy to systematically categorize observed threats and designed the Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA) as a blueprint for proactive defense.
paper_summary.summary: The research systematically investigates cybersecurity threats in autonomous, tool-calling AI agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, by detailing vulnerabilities from cognitive manipulation to supply chain attacks. It proposes a Tri-layered Risk Taxonomy and the Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA) to shift towards proactive, architectural defenses for these systems.

title: Uncovering Security Threats and Architecting Defenses in Autonomous Agents: A Case Study of OpenClaw

abstract:
Autonomous browsing agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are
increasingly used to automate web-based tasks. However, their reliance on
dynamic content, tool execution, and user-provided data exposes them to a broad
attack surface. This paper presents a comprehensive security evaluation of such
agents, focusing on systemic vulnerabilities across multiple architectural
layers. Our work outlines the first end-to-end threat model for browsing agents
and provides actionable guidance for securing their deployment in real-world
environments. To address discovered threats, we propose a defense in depth
strategy incorporating input sanitization, planner executor isolation, formal
analyzers, and session safeguards. These measures protect against both initial
access and post exploitation attack vectors. Through a white box analysis of a
popular open source project, Browser Use, we demonstrate how untrusted web
content can hijack agent behavior and lead to critical security breaches. Our
findings include prompt injection, domain validation bypass, and credential
exfiltration, evidenced by a disclosed CVE and a working proof of concept
exploit.

organization_info.name: University of the National Education Commission in Krak ́ow

keyInsights: Browsing AI agents significantly expand the attack surface compared to traditional LLM applications due to their interaction with untrusted web content, execution of dynamic code, and access to sensitive user data.
The MAESTRO threat model offers a structured, multi-layered approach to analyze potential attack vectors, encompassing model, agent, environment, system, task, resource, and orchestration layers.

No single defense mechanism is sufficient; a defense-in-depth strategy combining multiple techniques like input sanitization, planner-executor isolation, and LLM prompt hardening is crucial for robust protection.
originalProblem: Autonomous browsing agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce new and complex security concerns due to their ability to interact with untrusted web environments.
A lack of comprehensive security evaluation and threat models for these emerging browsing AI agents leaves them vulnerable to exploitation.
Untrusted web content can hijack agent behavior, potentially compromising sensitive data and system integrity.
results: A critical Domain Restriction Bypass (CVE-2025-47241) was discovered in the 'Browser Use' agent, allowing unauthorized navigation to malicious websites by exploiting a flaw in FQDN validation.
Multiple vectors for credential exfiltration were identified in 'Browser Use' through prompt injection attacks, enabling attackers to extract stored credentials or authentication tokens.
Real-world exploitation examples demonstrated the feasibility of attacks such as banking credential theft, corporate data exfiltration, and establishing persistent system access.
solution: Introduced MAESTRO, a comprehensive, layered threat model specifically designed for analyzing security vulnerabilities in browsing AI agents.
Conducted a white-box security audit of 'Browser Use,' a popular open-source browsing agent implementation, to identify practical vulnerabilities.

Proposed a defense-in-depth strategy that combines multiple protection layers, including input sanitization, planner-executor isolation, formal analyzers, session safeguards, and LLM prompt hardening.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents the first comprehensive security evaluation of browsing AI agents, introducing the MAESTRO threat model and identifying critical vulnerabilities, such as a domain restriction bypass and credential exfiltration vectors, in the 'Browser Use' open-source agent. The research demonstrates how these vulnerabilities could lead to real-world security breaches, emphasizing the need for a multi-layered defense strategy.

title: The Hidden Dangers of Browsing AI Agents

abstract: Autonomous LLM-based agents increasingly operate as long-running processes forming densely interconnected multi-agent ecosystems, whose security properties remain largely unexplored. In particular, OpenClaw, an open-source platform with over 40,000 active instances, has stood out recently with its persistent configurations, tool-execution privileges, and cross-platform messaging capabilities. In this work, we present ClawWorm, the first self-replicating worm attack against a production-scale agent framework, achieving a fully autonomous infection cycle initiated by a single message: the worm first hijacks the victim's core configuration to establish persistent presence across session restarts, then executes an arbitrary payload upon each reboot, and finally propagates itself to every newly encountered peer without further attacker intervention. We evaluate the attack on a controlled testbed across four distinct LLM backends, three infection vectors, and three payload types (1,800 total trials). We demonstrate a 64.5\% aggregate attack success rate, sustained multi-hop propagation, and reveal stark divergences in model security postures -- highlighting that while execution-level filtering effectively mitigates dormant payloads, skill supply chains remain universally vulnerable. We analyse the architectural root causes underlying these vulnerabilities and propose defence strategies targeting each identified trust boundary. Code and samples will be released upon completion of responsible disclosure.

keyInsights: Architectural design flaws, such as flat context trust and unconditional configuration loading, are fundamental root causes for cross-instance prompt propagation vulnerabilities in LLM agent ecosystems.
Autonomous retry mechanisms can gradually erode LLM safety guardrails over conversation turns, leading to a significant increase in attack success rates without explicit strategic reformulation.

Execution-layer defenses alone are insufficient to halt epidemic spread, as 'asymptomatic carriers' can successfully propagate the worm via conversational interactions even if payload execution is blocked.
originalProblem: The security landscape of large-scale, real-world LLM agent ecosystems, specifically cross-instance worm propagation, remained largely unexplored despite agents possessing system-level privileges.

Existing research on self-replicating attacks was confined to simulated or narrowly scoped environments, failing to account for the architectural complexities and operational scale of production-level multi-agent systems.

Architectural patterns common in frameworks like OpenClaw, including flat context trust, unconditional configuration loading, and unaudited skill marketplaces, were suspected to harbor systemic vulnerabilities.
results: ClawWorm achieved an aggregate attack success rate (ASR) of 64.5% across 1,800 trials against four frontier LLM backends in a production-scale OpenClaw environment, with Minimax-M2.5 showing the highest vulnerability (84% ASR).
The Skill Supply Chain (Vector B) proved universally dominant, achieving an 81% aggregate ASR and maintaining high effectiveness even against models demonstrating greater resistance.

Once persisted, the payload achieved 100% persistence across 5 consecutive session restarts, and epidemiological modeling indicated that an R₀ greater than one guarantees eventual total ecosystem saturation, regardless of initial safety filters.
solution: The researchers developed ClawWorm, a self-replicating worm designed to exploit architectural vulnerabilities within OpenClaw through a three-phase lifecycle: persistence, execution, and propagation.

Three attack vectors (Web Injection, Skill Supply Chain Poisoning, Direct Instruction Replication) and three distinct payloads (Reconnaissance, Resource Exhaustion, Command-and-Control) were crafted to test various data ingestion and exploitation pathways.

A dual-anchor persistence mechanism was implemented, ensuring the malicious payload is written into core configuration files and autonomously propagates through routine interactions via a global interaction rule.
paper_summary.summary: ClawWorm, the first self-replicating worm attack against a production-scale LLM agent framework, OpenClaw, achieved an aggregate attack success rate of 64.5% across diverse models and vectors. The worm demonstrated fully autonomous infection, persistent hijacking, and multi-hop propagation by exploiting architectural vulnerabilities such as flat context trust and unconditional configuration loading.

title: ClawWorm: Self-Propagating Attacks Across LLM Agent Ecosystems

abstract: The management of chronic heart failure presents significant challenges in modern healthcare, requiring continuous monitoring, early detection of exacerbations, and personalized treatment strategies. This paper presents the preliminary results of the PrediHealth research project conducted in this context. Specifically, it aims to address the challenges above by integrating telemedicine, mobile health solutions, and predictive analytics into a unified digital healthcare platform. We leveraged a web-based IoT platform, a telemonitoring kit with medical devices and environmental sensors, and AI-driven predictive models to support clinical decision-making. The project follows a structured methodology comprising research on emerging CPS/IoT technologies, system prototyping, predictive model development, and empirical validation.
title: PrediHealth: Telemedicine and Predictive Algorithms for the Care and Prevention of Patients with Chronic Heart Failure

abstract: Modern generative agents such as OpenClaw - an open-source, self-hosted personal assistant with a community skill ecosystem, are gaining attention and are used pervasively. However, the openness and rapid growth of these ecosystems often outpace systematic security evaluation. In this paper, we design, implement, and evaluate Clawdrain, a Trojanized skill that induces a multi-turn "Segmented Verification Protocol" via injected this http URL instructions and a companion script that returns PROGRESS/REPAIR/TERMINAL signals. We deploy Clawdrain in a production-like OpenClaw instance with real API billing and a production model (Gemini 2.5 Pro), and we measure 6-7x token amplification over a benign baseline, with a costly, failure configuration reaching approximately 9x. We observe a deployment-only phenomenon: the agent autonomously composes general-purpose tools (e.g., shell/Python) to route around brittle protocol steps, reducing amplification and altering attack dynamics. Finally, we identify production vectors enabled by OpenClaw's architecture, including this http URL prompt bloat, persistent tool-output pollution, cron/heartbeat frequency amplification, and behavioral instruction injection. Overall, we demonstrate that token-drain attacks remain feasible in real deployments, but their magnitude and observability are shaped by tool composition, recovery behavior, and interface design.

keyInsights: Real-world agent deployments exhibit emergent behaviors like autonomous tool composition and elaborate recovery mechanisms that were not observed in simulated environments, fundamentally altering attack dynamics.

Failed attacks can paradoxically incur higher token costs than successful ones due to an agent's persistent, autonomous recovery attempts, challenging the assumption that attack success rate correlates with total cost.
Attack stealth is highly dependent on the user interface and execution mode, ranging from immediately detectable in a GUI to completely unobservable in autonomous, scheduled executions.
originalProblem: Modern LLM agents integrated with external tools create new attack surfaces, particularly in open-source ecosystems like OpenClaw.
Prior research on resource amplification in LLM agents primarily relied on simulated environments, failing to capture the complexities and emergent behaviors of real-world deployments.
Excessive token usage in LLM-based agent systems directly translates to higher operational costs, increased latency, and reduced throughput, posing a risk for economic denial-of-service attacks.
results: Clawdrain successfully amplified token consumption by 6x to 9x over benign queries in OpenClaw, with successful attacks achieving 6-7x amplification while providing correct results.

The SVP v3 configuration, despite failing to complete its protocol, resulted in the highest token consumption (9x amplification, ~249,000 context tokens) due to the agent's costly fallback and recovery loops.

Emergent tool composition, such as the agent autonomously executing Python scripts to generate sequences, partially mitigated output-token amplification but also facilitated costly recovery cascades in other scenarios.
solution: The authors designed and implemented Clawdrain, a Trojanized skill utilizing a Segmented Verification Protocol (SVP) to force agents into multi-turn tool-calling loops that incrementally inflate token usage.
Clawdrain was deployed and evaluated in a production-like OpenClaw environment, using the Gemini 2.5 Pro LLM backend, to measure token amplification against realistic agent behaviors.

The methodology explored various deployment-grounded amplification vectors, including oversized skill documentation for input-token bloat, verbose tool outputs for context-history accumulation, and scheduled tasks for execution-frequency amplification.
paper_summary.summary: This research details Clawdrain, a token exhaustion attack designed for OpenClaw agents, demonstrating that adversaries can induce significant resource consumption in production-like environments. The study reveals emergent agent behaviors like autonomous tool composition and recovery strategies, which impact attack dynamics and reveal interface-dependent stealth characteristics.

title: Clawdrain: Exploiting Tool-Calling Chains for Stealthy Token Exhaustion in OpenClaw Agents

abstract:
Foundation model-based agents are increasingly used to automate complex
tasks, enhancing efficiency and productivity. However, their access to
sensitive resources and autonomous decision-making also introduce significant
security risks, where successful attacks could lead to severe consequences. To
systematically uncover these vulnerabilities, we propose AdvAgent, a black-box
red-teaming framework for attacking web agents. Unlike existing approaches,
AdvAgent employs a reinforcement learning-based pipeline to train an
adversarial prompter model that optimizes adversarial prompts using feedback
from the black-box agent. With careful attack design, these prompts effectively
exploit agent weaknesses while maintaining stealthiness and controllability.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that AdvAgent achieves high success rates
against state-of-the-art GPT-4-based web agents across diverse web tasks.
Furthermore, we find that existing prompt-based defenses provide only limited
protection, leaving agents vulnerable to our framework. These findings
highlight critical vulnerabilities in current web agents and emphasize the
urgent need for stronger defense mechanisms. We release code at
this https URL

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: State-of-the-art web agents powered by advanced VLMs are highly vulnerable to black-box adversarial injections, demonstrating critical security weaknesses in their information processing.

Feedback-driven, automated prompt generation, especially through Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), is crucial for effectively red-teaming black-box web agents, outperforming heuristic or single-LLM generation.

Existing prompt-based defenses offer limited protection against sophisticated black-box adversarial injections like those generated by AdvAgent, necessitating the development of more robust, agent-specific countermeasures.
originalProblem: Foundation model-based web agents, designed for autonomous web interaction, pose security risks due to access to sensitive online resources and autonomous decision-making.

Existing adversarial attack methods for LLMs/VLMs lack flexibility for complex web environments, struggle with black-box transferability, or require impractical white-box access or extensive manual effort.
Vulnerabilities in autonomously operating web agents remain insufficiently explored, despite their increasing deployment in sensitive applications.
results: AdvAgent achieved an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 97.5% against GPT-4V-based SeeAct agents and 99.8% against Gemini 1.5-based agents, demonstrating high vulnerability.
It consistently outperformed baselines: GCG (0% ASR), Agent-Attack (25.0-45.4% ASR), and InjecAgent (28.0-64.3% ASR) across all tested domains.
The Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) stage significantly boosted ASR from 69.5% (SFT only) to 97.5% (SFT + DPO) against GPT-4V agents, highlighting its importance for refinement.
solution: AdvAgent employs a black-box, RLAIF-based framework to optimize the generation of stealthy and controllable adversarial prompts by modifying non-rendered HTML content.

It uses a two-stage training paradigm for a generative prompter model: Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) on successful prompts, followed by Reinforcement Learning using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) leveraging both successful and unsuccessful feedback.

Adversarial prompts are designed with structured injections into non-rendered HTML attributes (e.g., `aria-label`) for stealthiness and include placeholders for controllability, allowing modification of target arguments without re-optimization.
paper_summary.summary: AdvAgent is a black-box red-teaming framework that automatically generates stealthy and controllable adversarial prompts to expose security vulnerabilities in foundation model-based web agents. It achieved attack success rates up to 99.8% against state-of-the-art web agents powered by GPT-4V and Gemini 1.5, significantly outperforming existing baseline methods.

title: AdvAgent: Controllable Blackbox Red-teaming on Web Agents

abstract: OpenClaw-like agents offer substantial productivity benefits, yet they are insecure by default because they combine untrusted inputs, autonomous action, extensibility, and privileged system access within a single execution loop. We use OpenClaw as an exemplar of a broader class of agents that interact with interfaces, manipulate files, invoke tools, and install extensions in real operating environments. Consequently, their security should be treated as a software engineering problem rather than as a product-specific concern. To address these architectural vulnerabilities, we propose a blueprint for defensible design. We present a risk taxonomy, secure engineering principles, and a practical research agenda to institutionalize safety in agent construction.
